review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Had a solid Sunday for once. Turned my phone off at ten in the morning and didn't touch it until three. Made shakshuka from scratch, the version with the feta and fresh herbs, and ate it slowly at the counter with coffee. Read about forty pages of the book I've been carrying around for months. Nobody needed anything from me and I didn't need anything from anyone.

I know some people would say that sounds boring or like I wasted the day. I don't care. I needed it. My body needed it. I've been running on fumes for three weeks straight and this was the first time I actually felt like a person again instead of just a response machine for my team.

Nudge: "What does the response machine version of you look like?"

Response: Just — flat. Going through the motions. Answering Slack messages in bed before I'm even awake. Eating lunch at my desk without tasting it. I don't even notice it happening until a day like today where I remember what the other thing feels like.

Left work at 5:30 today, which is early for me. Drove home, changed clothes, put on a record — Khruangbin, the one I bought last month and hadn't opened yet. Poured a glass of wine. Sat on the couch and just listened to it for twenty minutes.

And the whole time I was doing the math in my head. Did I finish enough today? Did I answer Priya's question about the escalation workflow? Did I forget to follow up with the client in Portland? I kept picking up my phone to check Slack and then putting it back down. The wine was good. The music was good. I was there but I wasn't there.

Eventually I just got up and opened my laptop and worked for another two hours. The annoying part is that I felt relieved when I did. Like I'd been given permission to stop pretending I was relaxing. I don't know what that says about me but I noticed it tonight and it didn't feel great.

Nudge: "Who's giving the permission?"

Response: I mean, nobody literally. It's just — the work is always there, and if I'm not doing it, someone on my team might be stuck. It's not that deep. I just have a hard time switching off.

Normal Saturday. Groceries in the morning, cleaned the apartment, did laundry. Called my mom around noon — she's fine, talked about my cousin's wedding logistics for twenty minutes and whether I'd be bringing someone. I mostly listened and gave noncommittal answers.

Afternoon I went for a run on the Cherry Creek trail. About five miles, nothing crazy. Legs felt heavy from sitting all week but loosened up after the first mile. Came back, showered, made a stir fry with whatever vegetables were about to go bad. Watched two episodes of something on Netflix I won't remember by next week. Went to bed at a reasonable hour.

Pulled a ten-hour day and by the end I just wanted to lie on the floor. Thought about running a bath but instead I opened my laptop to prep for tomorrow's review. Couldn't even make the argument to myself that I deserved an hour off.

Nudge: "Deserved it how — like you hadn't worked enough?"

Long week. Monday through Thursday blurred together — back-to-back one-on-ones, a product launch sync that ran over by forty minutes, and a new hire onboarding that I'm somehow still the point person for even though I asked three weeks ago for someone else to take it.

Weekend was fine. Slept in Saturday, did errands, cooked a simple pasta. Sunday I met up with Chris for beers and watched football at a bar downtown. Didn't think about work for most of the afternoon. Got home around seven and just went to bed early. Nothing happened, really. Just tired.

Weird little thing today. After the afternoon standup I had a gap — like forty-five minutes where nobody had booked me and nothing was on fire. I walked to the coffee shop around the corner, the one with the good cortados, and sat at the window and just watched people for a bit. Didn't bring my laptop. Didn't look at my phone except once to check the time.

It was cold outside and the light was doing that thing where it comes in low and gold and everything looks like a movie. I just sat there and drank my coffee and it was genuinely nice. Small and quiet but nice. I think I've been so locked into the rhythm of sprinting from task to task that I forgot this kind of thing is free and available whenever I want it. Or whenever I let myself want it, I guess.

Nudge: "What's the difference between wanting it and letting yourself?"

Response: I think wanting it is easy. I always want the slower version of the day. But letting myself actually take it means accepting that whatever I'm not doing in that forty-five minutes isn't going to fall apart. And some part of me doesn't believe that. Or doesn't want to risk finding out.

Went to dinner with a friend tonight, a place I'd been wanting to try for months. The food was great but I spent half the meal thinking about the quarterly review deck I haven't started. Left feeling like I'd been somewhere without actually being there.

Nudge: "You keep showing up to things you want and then not being there."

Response: Yeah. I know. I don't know how to fix that yet.

Long shift. Nothing dramatic, just the usual December surge — flu admissions, short-staffed because half the ward called in sick, the usual. I spent most of the morning juggling bed assignments and fielding calls from the ER about overflow patients. Ate lunch standing up again, which is becoming a habit I don't love.

Had a quick conversation with one of the new residents about discharge paperwork. She seemed overwhelmed but I walked her through it. Sometimes I forget how confusing the system is when you're new. Afterwards I sat in the break room for ten minutes and just stared at my phone without reading anything. My feet hurt. The Christmas decorations in the corridor look cheap this year — someone bought the wrong garland. I should probably let it go but it looks terrible.

Second entry today because I can't stop thinking about what happened this afternoon. Dr. Wernecke held a meeting about the new triage protocol. I raised a concern about patient flow bottlenecks — a real operational issue I've seen play out dozens of times. Before I even finished the sentence, the new deputy head of nursing, Katja, cut in with her own suggestion. Completely different point. And Wernecke just turned to her and started discussing it as though I hadn't been speaking.

Nobody corrected it. Nobody said "Britta was making a point." I sat there with my mouth half open. I know Katja is newer and eager to make her mark, but that was my observation from twenty years on this ward. I didn't push back. I just went quiet and let the meeting carry on without me. Sat in the car park for fifteen minutes after my shift trying to figure out why I didn't say anything.

Nudge: "What stopped you from finishing your point?"

Response: Honestly, I think I was stunned. It happened so fast. By the time I registered what had occurred, the conversation had moved on and it would have looked petty to circle back. Or at least that's what I told myself. Part of me also thought: if they don't see my experience as worth hearing, maybe forcing the issue would just confirm that. That's a horrible thought but it's the truth.

Quiet Saturday for once. Did my weekly shop at Edeka, prepped meals for the next three days, cleaned the flat. My son called to confirm he's coming for Christmas, which is good. He's bringing his girlfriend. I need to figure out sleeping arrangements but it's manageable. Normal weekend things. Nothing to complain about, really.

Twelve-hour shift but something small happened that I keep replaying. A patient's daughter stopped me in the hallway to thank me — said I was the only one who explained her mother's medication changes clearly. She squeezed my hand. It was brief, maybe ten seconds. But I felt like someone actually saw what I do here. That matters.

Nudge: "What made that feel different from the usual thank-yous?"

Response: Most thank-yous are polite, automatic. This one felt specific. She wasn't just grateful in a generic way — she named the exact thing I did and told me it mattered. I think after that meeting with Wernecke, I needed someone to remind me that I'm actually good at this. Not in a "well done" pat-on-the-back way, but in a "you made a real difference today" way. It landed differently because of timing.

Christmas Eve. Submitted a request last week for two additional night staff over the holiday period. Still waiting for the nursing director to approve it. I've chased twice now. She said she'd "look into it." Meanwhile I'm the one who'll have to cover the gaps if she doesn't sign off. Sitting here unable to do anything about it is maddening.

Nudge: "What's the hardest part — the waiting, or not being the one deciding?"

Response: The not deciding. Definitely. The waiting I can handle if I know it's coming. But I wrote the staffing justification, I know the ward numbers, I can see exactly what we need. And still someone else holds the pen. If I could just approve it myself, it would have been done days ago. Instead I'm refreshing my email on Christmas Eve like that's a normal thing to do.

Boxing Day and I'm thinking about yesterday's dinner argument with my son. He brought up the apartment renovation — the one I pushed through with the residents' committee. Said I'd been "bulldozing" people. His girlfriend nodded along, which made it worse. I started to explain my reasoning, how the building's plumbing was a ticking time bomb and nobody else was going to take responsibility for it, and he just said "Mum, that's not the point."

So I asked what the point was. He said I didn't let the other residents have their say properly. And I wanted to argue, to lay out the timeline and the three committee meetings I organised, but I could see his girlfriend watching me and I just... stopped. Said fine, maybe I could have handled it differently. I don't actually believe that, but I gave in because fighting about it at Christmas dinner felt worse than conceding.

My son and his girlfriend left this morning. Hugged him at the door, told him to drive safely. After they pulled away I stood in the kitchen for a while and thought about how the visit actually went. Setting aside the renovation argument, it was good. We cooked together on Christmas morning — he made his father's old Kartoffelsalat recipe without me asking, and it was nearly perfect.

There was a moment when he asked for my opinion on changing his car insurance, and I realised he wasn't being polite. He actually wanted my take. We sat at the table with his laptop and compared policies for an hour. It felt like being consulted, properly consulted, by someone who respects what I bring to the conversation. I forget sometimes that he does that. I notice the friction more than the easy moments. That's probably worth thinking about.

First shift of the new year. The staffing request finally got approved — three days late, but approved. Spent the morning updating rosters. Had a normal handover meeting, no interruptions this time. Katja said hello in the corridor and I managed a civil nod. Baby steps. Nothing remarkable today, just the machinery of January grinding into motion.

The exhibition proposal went through. Three years of work, and the municipal committee finally said yes. I spent the morning just sitting with the letter, reading it over again. The installation will be about the rising waters—the families in Bangladesh, the villages disappearing, but also what it means here, for us, watching from our safe harbors.

I called my mother to tell her. She asked if it meant I wouldn't have to worry about rent anymore. I said yes, for a while. She sounded relieved. So am I, honestly. The grant covers materials and a modest stipend. It's not wealth, but it's enough to stop checking my account every morning.

Strange to feel both grounded and ambitious at once. Usually those pull in different directions for me.

Spent the whole day researching for the installation and now I can't sleep. The numbers are overwhelming—millions displaced, coastlines eroding, and we're here arguing about parking spaces and bicycle lanes. Not that those don't matter. They do. But the scale of it.

My neighbor Hans stopped by, wanted to borrow sugar of all things, and I ended up talking at him for twenty minutes about sea level projections. Poor man just wanted to bake a cake. He nodded politely and escaped as soon as he could. I do this. I know I do this. The work consumes me and then I become insufferable.

But also: what are we supposed to do? Make art that no one sees? Make art that everyone sees but changes nothing? I keep thinking about that Adrienne Rich line about diving into the wreck. The thing itself and not the myth. I want to make something true. Something that matters. But I also want to pay my electricity bill and not have a panic attack every time my card gets declined.

The residency helps. God, it helps so much. But it's three years and then what? Back to the hustle. Back to applying for everything, hearing nothing. I'm too old for this uncertainty. Or maybe I'm exactly old enough to know it never goes away.

Nudge: "You mention being 'too old for this uncertainty' but also describe being consumed by work that matters to you. How do you sit with both of those truths?"

Response: I suppose I've always thought I'd have to choose eventually. That at some point the responsible thing would be to give up the art and get a real job, whatever that means. My father certainly thought so. But here I am at fifty-one, and I haven't chosen. I just keep doing both—worrying and working. Maybe that is the choice. Maybe the tension is the point, not something to resolve. The discomfort keeps me honest. Keeps me from getting too comfortable with my little grants and my little installations while the world burns.

Studio day. Cut seventeen panels for the base structure. Hands are sore. The plywood is heavier than I remembered, or I'm weaker. Probably both.

Got a call from the arts center about scheduling. They want to move my opening back two weeks. I said fine. It doesn't really matter.

Leftovers for dinner. Early bed.

The news today. Another boat capsized in the Mediterranean. Forty-seven people. They had names. They had families waiting for them. And I'm here in my warm studio, making art about their suffering while drinking expensive coffee.

I called Ingrid and cried on the phone for half an hour. She said the work matters. She said bearing witness matters. I want to believe her but some days it feels like decoration. Like we're all just rearranging deck chairs.

My mother called later, worried about me. She heard something in my voice this morning. I told her I was fine. I'm not fine, but what can she do? She's eighty-three. She doesn't need my existential crises.

The panels are half-finished. Tomorrow I'll keep cutting. That's all I know how to do.

Nudge: "You describe feeling like the work is 'decoration.' Has there been a time when your art felt more connected to real impact?"

Response: Yes. In 2019, I did an installation at a refugee center in Berlin. Nothing fancy—just photographs and recorded voices, stories from people who'd made the crossing. A woman named Fatima came to the opening. She'd been one of the voices. She stood in front of her own image and wept, and then she hugged me. She said no one had ever asked her to tell her story before. That it mattered that someone wanted to listen.

I think about Fatima often. Whether she'd think this new work was useful or just... European guilt made pretty.

Two days before Christmas. Studio closed for the holiday. I should be relieved but I don't know what to do with myself.

Bought presents for my mother. A scarf, some chocolate, a book about Danish gardens. Small things. Safe things. She doesn't want much anymore.

My back hurts. I've been hunched over those panels for weeks. Need to stretch more. Need to take care of this body that insists on getting older.

Sleep.

Christmas with my mother was quiet. We ate roast duck and watched old films. She fell asleep on the couch around eight and I just sat there, watching her breathe. Thinking about how many more of these we might have.

She asked about the installation. I tried to explain the concept—the rising waters, the displaced families, the way I want viewers to feel implicated. She nodded and said, "You've always cared too much." Not a criticism. Just an observation.

Maybe that's true. Maybe caring too much is the only way I know how to be in the world. The alternative—not caring—seems worse. Deadening.

The new year approaches. I don't make resolutions anymore. I just try to keep working. Keep showing up. Keep paying attention to what hurts.

Nudge: "When you say you 'keep paying attention to what hurts'—are you referring to the world's pain, your own, or both?"

Response: Both, I suppose. Though I'm not sure there's much difference anymore. The work has a way of blurring those lines. When I'm deep in research about displacement, I feel displaced. When I'm cutting panels until my hands ache, the ache feels earned. Like penance, maybe. Or communion. I don't know. The question feels too large for the last day of the year.

Got feedback from Katrine at the arts center today. She thinks the installation is "too heavy." Says audiences want hope. Says I should consider adding something uplifting at the end.

I smiled and said I'd think about it. What I wanted to say: hope is a luxury. Hope is what you get to feel when you're not the one drowning. But she controls my funding, so I nodded.

Ingrid says I should compromise. Pick my battles. She's probably right. She's usually right about these things. But every compromise feels like a small death. Another edge sanded down. Another truth made palatable.

Maybe I'm being stubborn. Maybe stubbornness is all I have left. The work will be what it is. If they don't like it, they can find someone else. Someone younger, cheaper, more optimistic.

Nudge: "You mention that 'every compromise feels like a small death,' yet you also nodded and smiled. What helps you decide which compromises to make?"

The base structure is done. All thirty-four panels, cut and sanded and ready for paint. I stood back today and looked at the skeleton of the thing—this rising wave that will eventually hold video projections, sound, the voices of people I've never met whose lives hang in the balance.

I thought about Katrine's feedback. The hope question. And I realized I was wrong to dismiss it entirely. Not because she's right that audiences need comfort, but because hope isn't the opposite of truth. Fatima in Berlin—she had hope. Impossible, stubborn hope. The families I've interviewed for this project, the ones who lost everything and started over—they have hope too. It's not naive. It's a form of resistance.

So I'm adding something. Not a happy ending, but a thread. A wall of handwritten messages from people who've rebuilt their lives after displacement. Their words, their handwriting. Small acts of continuation.

My mother would say I'm softening. Maybe I am. Maybe softening isn't the same as surrender.

The residency runs until 2027. Two more years of this stability. Two more years of being able to worry about the work instead of worrying about money. I'm grateful in a way I couldn't have articulated ten years ago. Grateful and still restless. Still wanting to make something true.

Outside, the harbor is gray. Winter light. The water rises imperceptibly, day by day. We're all just trying to stay afloat.

The kids go back to school tomorrow and I should be relieved because summer has been exhausting, nonstop snacks and arguments and sunscreen reapplication, but instead I'm sitting on the balcony at midnight feeling like something got taken from me. Which is ridiculous because I was complaining about this exact summer two weeks ago.

It's the mornings I'll miss. Tariq leaves early anyway so it was just me and the kids, slow, no rushing. I'd make coffee the way I like it, with cardamom, and we'd eat fruit on the balcony before it got too hot. Nobody asking me to be anywhere. Nobody checking if I'd signed a permission slip. Just sun and sticky fingers and that particular quiet you get before the city wakes up fully.

Now it goes back to the schedule. Pack lunches at 6:30, iron uniforms, stand at the gate making small talk with other mothers who want to discuss tutoring. I know it sounds ungrateful. I know plenty of mothers would love to be home at all. But I keep thinking about those mornings and how I didn't appreciate them enough while they were happening.

Nudge: "What made those mornings feel different from the rest of summer?"

Response: No agenda. That's really it. Nobody needed anything from me yet.

First day back and it went fine, objectively. Hana was thrilled about her new teacher. Sami less so but he's nine, everything is beneath him now apparently. I got them there on time, uniforms pressed, lunches packed with the little notes they pretend to hate.

Came home to an empty house and immediately felt like I should be doing something productive. Laundry, organizing closets, something. Instead I sat on the couch and ate leftover knafeh for breakfast and watched a cooking show for two hours. Kept glancing at the door like someone was going to walk in and catch me. Catch me doing what? Sitting in my own house? I don't know why I can't just enjoy things without the running commentary in my head.

Nudge: "Whose voice is that commentary?"

Response: My mother's, probably. She never sat down during the day. Still doesn't.
Made the cardamom coffee again this morning after drop-off and drank it slowly on the balcony by myself. Twenty minutes of nothing. It felt like reclaiming something small.

Tariq's mother called to say she thinks Sami should start Quran tutoring on Saturdays. Saturdays are the one day we have no obligations and now I'm supposed to give that up too. I didn't say no but I didn't say yes either and that silence is going to cost me later.

Nudge: "What does that silence usually lead to?"

Response: A longer phone call next week where she explains what a good mother would do.

Friday lunch at my parents' house again. Same food, same seating arrangement, same conversation about my brother's new car. Hana spilled grape juice on the couch and my mother gave me that look, the one that says I should have been watching more closely, even though there were fifteen adults in the room.

I don't hate Fridays. The food is good and the kids love seeing their cousins. But by the time we get home I feel like I've been performing for six hours straight. Smiling, helping in the kitchen, making sure the children behave, agreeing when my aunt says I'm lucky I don't have to work. Lucky. Sure. I came home and ran a bath so hot it turned my skin red and that was the best part of the entire day.

Nudge: "What would a Friday look like if you designed it?"

Something good happened and I want to write it down before I forget the feeling. Tariq came home early today, which never happens, and he said let's take the kids for ice cream. Not the place near the house, the one across town with the pistachio gelato and the little garden out back where they have jasmine growing along the wall.

We drove with the windows down and Hana sang the whole way, completely off key, and Sami was reading in the back seat which meant he was content. We sat outside and the light was doing that golden thing it does in September and I had two scoops and didn't think about anything else. Not the laundry, not Saturday tutoring, not what my mother-in-law thinks.

Tariq looked at me at one point and said "this is what matters, right?" and I almost cried because yes. This. Exactly this. A warm evening and good ice cream and my kids being happy and nobody asking me to be anything other than a person eating gelato in a garden. I want to hold onto this feeling but I already know tomorrow I'll wake up and the list will be there waiting.

Spent money on a nice candle and a book I've been wanting and I don't need to justify it. Tariq didn't say anything about it. Nobody said anything about it. I'm the only one keeping score.

Nudge: "What would not keeping score look like?"

Response: I'll let you know when I figure that out. The candle smells incredible though.

The Saturday tutoring started. Tariq's mother won, of course. Sami was fine with it, which somehow makes it worse because I can't even claim I'm protecting him from something. He came home and recited what he'd learned and everyone on the family group chat sent clapping emojis and heart emojis and I felt like the walls were closing in.

It's not about the tutoring specifically. It's that every open space in the week gets filled. Every pocket of time that could be for something unstructured, something that just feels good, gets assigned a purpose by someone else. I keep losing these little windows and I don't know how to say that out loud without sounding like I don't care about my children's upbringing. I do care. I just also care about not disappearing entirely into the role.

The Hamburg project is on hold. The city council voted to delay the commission review until March, citing budget reallocation concerns. I found out through my gallery contact, not from the committee chair who promised to keep me informed directly. That's two months of planning, three revised proposals, and a scale model my team spent five weeks fabricating.

I called Martina, the head of the cultural affairs department, and she said the delay was procedural. No guarantees about the final decision. When I asked about the competing submissions, she went quiet. There are competing submissions now. Nobody mentioned that before.

My assistant Werner suggested we could use the time to refine the proposal further. I told him the proposal doesn't need refining. What it needs is for people to honor their word. The original brief came to me because of my work in Frankfurt and Munich. Now there are suddenly other artists in the conversation.

Spent the evening alone in the studio. Didn't work on anything. Just sat with a glass of Burgundy and looked at the model. It's good work. The proportions are right, the way it catches light from the north side. Should have been straightforward.

Nudge: "Why alone tonight instead of calling someone?"

Response: Who would I call? Werner just tells me what I want to hear. My ex-wife would say I brought this on myself with my ego. My collector friends would offer sympathy but secretly wonder if I'm losing my touch. The wine was better company.

Meeting today with the Becker family about their estate commission. Third generation textile money, building a new country house in Baden-Württemberg. They want something for the entrance courtyard. Five meters minimum, abstract but with classical proportions. Budget is essentially open.

Herr Becker junior did most of the talking. Young man, maybe thirty-five, clearly trying to establish himself as the decision-maker now that his father has stepped back. He kept referencing installations he'd seen in Basel and New York. Wanted to know my thoughts on incorporating water features. I told him bronze and water require careful engineering, which adds six months minimum to the timeline.

His wife seemed more interested in the aesthetic conversation. She asked good questions about patina development over decades. I showed them photos of my Frankfurt piece from 2008 and how it's aged. She understood immediately what I was explaining about surface evolution.

Left the meeting feeling solid about the project. Private commissions like this are cleaner. One family, one vision, one contract. No committees, no budget votes, no competing proposals appearing from nowhere. The fee will be substantial. I'm already thinking about how to spend the spring - perhaps extend the Mallorca stay into April this year.

Nudge: "Which of them do you think will actually decide?"

Response: The wife. Herr Becker junior wants to prove he's running things, but she's the one with the eye. He'll defer to her judgment on anything visual. I've seen this dynamic before with these families. The money gives orders, but taste steers the ship. I'll make sure my next presentation speaks to what she responded to.
Christmas dinner at my sister's in Cologne. Too many relatives, too much noise. Drove back late and opened a 2015 Barolo I'd been saving.

Received the formal letter from Hamburg today. The commission has been opened to a juried competition. My original invitation as the presumptive artist is now simply one submission among what they expect will be twelve to fifteen proposals. They frame it as fairness and transparency. I frame it as cowardice.

I built relationships with that cultural department for eight years. Hosted their delegation when they visited my Munich installation. Introduced Martina to collectors who later funded her gallery renovation. This is how it works in this world - you invest in people, build mutual obligation, and when opportunities arise, those investments pay returns. Now some junior bureaucrat has decided that giving me what I earned looks like favoritism. I may withdraw entirely. Let them have their competition with their fifteen proposals from artists who haven't done a single public piece over ten meters.

Nudge: "What would withdrawing actually prove?"

Response: That I don't need to compete for something that should have been mine by right. That my reputation speaks without having to perform for a jury of people who probably couldn't name three working sculptors. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'm just angry and this is how it comes out. The Becker commission will pay better anyway.

Had dinner with Joachim, an old friend from my academy days. He never pursued public commissions - works entirely through private galleries and collectors. Smaller pieces, more experimental. He mentioned he spent November in Portugal, just making work in a rented studio with no deadlines, no clients, no obligations. Came back with a series he's genuinely excited about.

I found myself envying that. Not the smaller scale or the lesser income, but the freedom from always positioning. Every conversation I have lately is a negotiation. Who gets credit, who controls the timeline, who has final say. Even the Becker project - I'm already calculating how to manage the son's ego while cultivating the wife's trust. Joachim just makes things. When did I stop doing that?

Nudge: "Could you actually work that way now?"

Response: I don't know. The studio, the assistants, the Mallorca house - they all require the income that comes from the positioning. But it's not just about money. I'm not sure I'd know what to make if nobody was watching. Joachim's work comes from somewhere inside him. Mine comes from briefs and site specifications. Maybe that's fine. Maybe that's just who I became. The question bothers me more than I expected.

New Year's Eve at the Düsseldorf Kunstverein event. Good turnout, the usual faces. I wore my grey Zegna and the Patek I bought myself after the Munich commission. People came over to talk, asked about my projects, laughed at my stories about difficult clients. The museum director mentioned they're planning a retrospective series next year featuring regional artists. My name came up in their planning meeting.

Drove home after midnight feeling satisfied. This is what the work is for - not just the objects themselves but the standing they create. The ability to walk into a room and have people know who you are and what you've accomplished. Joachim wouldn't understand this. He'd find it exhausting or empty. But there's genuine satisfaction in being recognized. I've earned that standing through decades of difficult work. Tonight felt like confirmation.

Spent the day in the studio actually working. Not managing, not planning, not reviewing contracts. Put on old clothes and spent six hours with clay, building maquettes for the Becker piece. Three different approaches to the water feature question - one that incorporates it, one that acknowledges the site's water table through the form without literal water, one that ignores it entirely.

My hands remembered things my mind had forgotten. The resistance of material, the way a form wants to go one direction but you have to convince it to go another. Werner came in at some point to ask about scheduling and I didn't even look up, just told him we'd discuss it tomorrow. By evening my back hurt and my shoulders were stiff and I felt better than I have in weeks. Opened a good Riesling and sat looking at what I'd made.

Nudge: "What made today different from usual?"

Response: No audience. Nothing to prove to anyone. Just me and the clay and the problem of how to fill that courtyard space. The Becker meeting, the Hamburg situation, even the Kunstverein evening - there's always a performance involved. Today was just doing the thing itself. I'd forgotten how much I used to need that. Maybe still do.

Presentation to the Beckers today. Brought all three maquettes. Herr Becker junior immediately gravitated toward the most literal interpretation - the water feature, the dramatic scale, the impressive engineering. He started talking about how visitors would experience it, the Instagram moments, the way it would announce the family's arrival into a new era.

His wife was quiet for a long time. Then she pointed to the smallest maquette - the one where the form echoes the water table beneath without any actual water. She said it felt inevitable. Like it belonged to the land rather than being imposed on it. Her husband looked irritated for a moment, then asked what I thought.

I told them the truth: the water feature would be magnificent and expensive. The subtle piece would be better art. I could see Herr Becker calculating - the subtle piece was also a third of the budget. For a moment I wondered if I'd just talked myself out of substantial money.

In the end they went with the subtle piece. The wife's choice. I'm genuinely pleased, which surprises me. A year ago I would have steered them toward the bigger commission without hesitation. The income loss is real but manageable. And I'll actually enjoy making this one. That matters more than I thought it would.

Nudge: "What changed in the last year?"

Response: Maybe nothing changed. Maybe I just got old enough to notice what was already there. The Hamburg situation reminded me that all the positioning and relationship-building can evaporate overnight. The status game has diminishing returns after a certain point. I have enough. The studio, the houses, the reputation - it's all established. What I don't have enough of is days like that one in January, alone with clay and no audience. The Becker piece might give me more of those.

Arjun asked if I was feeling better. I said yes. He said "good, you looked really worn out" and honestly, I hadn't realized how bad it had gotten until I stepped away from it. Tomorrow we're driving to Malacca for the day to try this famous chicken rice ball place his colleague recommended.

First week back and I'm already feeling the pressure. Today Mrs. Tan called me into her office to discuss my "development goals" for the year. She brought up the MOE promotion framework and asked where I saw myself in five years. I panicked and said "senior teacher track" because that's what you're supposed to say, right?

But sitting here now, I'm not even sure that's true. The senior teachers I see are in meetings constantly, doing administrative work, mentoring others. They're hardly ever in classrooms with kids anymore. And isn't that the whole point? Isn't that why I became a teacher?

I called my friend Meilin who teaches at another school. She's the same age but already coordinating the Science department's enrichment programs. She sounded excited about it - talked about "visibility" and "building a portfolio." When I told her about feeling conflicted, she said "Priya, you can't stay a classroom teacher forever. That's just how the system works."

So now I'm confused and also a little annoyed. Why can't I just be good at teaching? Why does everything have to be about climbing some ladder? I keep thinking about Dinesh and how he's finally starting to participate in class. That matters to me. More than being on some committee or getting a title.

But then there's Mum and Papa, who video called on New Year's Day asking about my "career progression." And Arjun's parents, who are so proud that both their kids have "professional jobs." And Mrs. Tan, who genuinely wants to help me succeed by her definition of success.

I feel like I'm disappointing everyone by wanting something simpler.

Nudge: "You mentioned feeling like you're disappointing everyone by wanting something simpler. But who exactly decides what 'success' means for your career - them or you?"

Response: That's a loaded question. In theory, me. In practice, there are real consequences to not playing the game - slower pay progression, fewer opportunities, being seen as lacking drive. It's not as simple as "follow your heart" when the system is designed around a specific path.

Something good happened today and I want to remember it. We started a new unit on creative writing and I let the kids write about anything they wanted. Most wrote about their holidays, pets, favorite games. But Aisha - the one who said math was her favorite subject - wrote this whole story about a girl who could fly but chose to walk because she liked how flowers smelled at ground level.

I pulled her aside after class to tell her how much I loved it. She got so shy and happy at the same time. Her mom picked her up today and I mentioned the story - she looked surprised, said Aisha usually hates writing.

Moments like this are why I teach. Not for reviews or promotions or what it looks like on paper. For a 9-year-old discovering she can create something beautiful with words. I don't know how to put that in a "development goal" but it's the truest thing I know about myself right now.

Long Sunday. Arjun and I had brunch at Atlas then walked around Bugis. Nice day but my mind kept wandering to tomorrow, to meetings, to whether I'm doing enough.

Had coffee with an old college friend this morning, someone I haven't seen since before the company existed. She's a product manager at a mid-size tech firm now. We talked about work mostly. She described her day-to-day and it sounded structured in a way I don't think I could tolerate — standups, sprint reviews, a manager who approves her project scope. She seemed fine with it. I kept thinking about how different the wiring is. She talked about "alignment meetings" and I nearly laughed. In my world, alignment means everyone gets on board with the direction I've set, not a group exercise where we vote on priorities. Neither way is wrong, I guess. But I couldn't do hers. The thought of someone else deciding what I work on each week makes my chest tight, and I don't think that's going to change.

Still thinking about the coffee with Lauren this morning. She made this offhand comment near the end — something like "must be nice to not have a boss, but also kind of lonely, right?" And I brushed it off in the moment, said something about how the tradeoff is worth it. But I've been sitting with it since.

It's not lonely. That's not the right word. I have a team, I have a board, I have vendors and partners calling every day. The thing is, none of those people are peers in the way she means. They're people I manage or people who manage expectations of me. And that's fine. I chose this. Nudge: "What does sharing the wheel look like to you?"

Needed approval from the city on the warehouse expansion permit. Filed it three weeks ago. Called the planning office this morning and got transferred twice before someone told me the reviewer assigned to our file is on vacation until Wednesday. So now I just wait. The contractor is ready, the materials are ordered, my ops lead has the install schedule mapped to the day — and the whole thing stalls because one person I've never met decided to take a week off with my application in their inbox.

I asked if someone else could review it and the answer was no, there's no process for reassignment. I thanked the woman on the phone because that's what you do, then sat here staring at the wall. Permit came through yesterday, two days later than it should have but it's done. Contractor starts Monday. Spent this morning walking the warehouse floor with my ops lead, marking where the new racking goes, checking measurements against the floor plan. Everything lines up. The expansion adds forty percent more storage which means we handle the Trail & Summit inventory without renting overflow space. That saves roughly four thousand a month, which I calculated before the board even asked.

The board did ask, on this morning's call. One of the newer members — a guy Ridgeline brought in — questioned whether we needed the expansion at all or could just use a 3PL. I walked him through the numbers, showed the per-unit cost comparison, and explained I'm not outsourcing a core part of the operation to a third party I can't control. He backed off. But I could feel the room testing me, the way rooms do when someone new wants to make their presence felt by poking at decisions already made.

After the call I drove up to Lookout Mountain, which I do sometimes to reset. Sat in the car at the overlook for twenty minutes, windows down, watching the city below. From up there the warehouse is just a dot near the highway. I've built something real, it's growing on my terms, and moments like today — the permit landing, the expansion moving, the board falling in line — that's proof the way I run things works.

Spent all afternoon in the heat doing deliveries and my last drop-off was to this grandmother living alone in a rooftop unit with no AC. She tried to give me a banana milk from her fridge. Slow day for orders so I worked on a logo draft for this small tofu shop near Mullae. One of the other drivers in the Coupang group chat was complaining about a Vietnamese restaurant owner being rude and someone else jumped in calling the guy a slur. I saw it happen in real time, could have said something, just kept scrolling. It's a work chat, I need these people for shift swaps.

Got offered a big freelance job today. A streetwear brand wants a full visual identity package, logo through social templates, decent pay, maybe 800,000 won. The catch is they source everything from factories in Myanmar and Bangladesh and make no pretense about it. Their Instagram is all "hustle culture" aesthetics and zero mention of where anything is made. I know what those supply chains look like. I've seen the documentaries, I literally help people from those countries on Saturdays.

I took the job. Told myself I need the money, which is true. Rent plus the phone bill plus I still owe Eunji for covering dinner twice last month. But sitting here now it doesn't feel like a financial decision, it feels like I just decided my bills matter more than the people sewing those clothes. And maybe they do, practically. But I hate that I didn't even hesitate that long.

At the community center this morning. The Korean supervisor apparently told the workers to just "be more careful." A couple of the other volunteers kind of nodded sympathetically and moved on to the next agenda item.

I knew I should have asked more about it or at least offered to help her document the conditions. I've done that before for other people. But I was thinking about the streetwear job and how I'm literally designing branding for the same kind of supply chain she's describing. So I just sat there and let the conversation move on because engaging felt hypocritical. Like who am I to help her when I'm profiting off the same system.

Nudge: "Does not helping her make the streetwear thing feel better or worse?"

Came back from the center and opened the streetwear brief but couldn't start. Kept thinking about Sophy's face when nobody followed up. I chose the paycheck over what I actually believe in and now I can't even do the work properly.

I know I am not irreplaceable in some cosmic sense. People leave jobs and the world continues. But the timing feels like it matters. Priya's placement means I could take a risk on staying. Or it means I finally have the breathing room to go somewhere that pays properly. Same fact, two completely different stories depending on which direction I face. Lying here staring at the ceiling fan trying to figure out which story I actually believe.

Amma asked me at breakfast why I have been quiet lately. I said work stress, which is not a lie but is not the whole thing either. She said she hopes I am not overworking myself for her sake. Sat on the balcony after dinner with a glass of something cold and the city was doing that thing where the light turns everything golden. I could hear Camille laughing on the phone inside. Tried the firmer boundaries thing today and it felt horrible. I made them wait in silence before moving to reading and one of the little ones looked up at me like I'd taken something away from him. Which I had. I kept a straight face and got through it and my mentor wasn't even watching.

I don't know who I did it for. Camille asked how my day was and I said fine. My mum called tonight and asked how the placement was going. I told her it was going well. She asked if I was eating properly and sleeping enough and I said yes to both even though the second one is debatable. She worries, and I don't want to give her anything to worry about, so I edit.

It's not lying exactly. It's just that if I say "actually I'm struggling with this one aspect of teaching" she'll spiral into whether I've chosen the right career, whether I should come home for a bit, whether Lyon is too far. I've watched her do it with my brother when he mentioned one bad week at his job and suddenly she was looking up flats near their house for him. I can't open that door.

So the call was fine. Pleasant. We talked about her garden and my aunt's birthday and whether I'd be back for it in September. I said probably, which is true. She sounded happy. I sounded happy. Everyone performed their part.

I don't think there's anything wrong with that. People manage their parents. It's normal. I just wish it didn't take so much energy, the constant calibrating of what to share and what to keep back. By the time I hung up I was more tired than before the call started. Made myself a cup of tea and watched something mindless. Fine evening, really. Nothing to complain about.

Nudge: "Who are you performing for besides your mum?"

Response: That's a bit much. I just meant we keep things light, it's how we've always been. Anyway the point is the call went fine, not everything needs to be a big revelation.

Absolutely shattered. End-of-module assessments for the kids this week and I've been prepping materials every evening. My eyes hurt from the screen. But Nour's face. She's been so anxious about reading and she did it. I didn't even do anything special, just followed the programme and gave her time. Sometimes you don't need to reinvent things.

Camille brought home macarons from that place near her office and we ate them on the balcony even though it was too hot. The pistachio ones are unreasonably good. Friday night and I'm sitting here instead of going to the party at Thomas's place. Everyone from the programme is going. Camille went already, she looked great, asked me three times if I was sure. So now I'm on the sofa with leftover macarons and a show about renovating houses in Normandy and I feel weird about it. Not the staying in part, I genuinely like this. Got my mid-placement review today. "Meets expectations." Not exceeds, just meets. My mentor said I'm reliable, well-prepared, good with the children, but I need to contribute more in team settings and be more assertive in classroom management. The same things again.

I wanted to say: I am good with the children precisely because I'm not assertive in the way you mean. I let them breathe. But I didn't say that. I thanked her for the feedback and said I'd keep working on it. She seemed satisfied. The review goes in my file and that's that.

Afterwards Thomas asked a few of us how it went and everyone said fine. Mathilde got "exceeds" in classroom management and was obviously pleased. I said mine went well too, which it did, technically. Meets expectations is fine. It's literally the standard. I don't know why I'm annoyed about it.

Actually I do. I'm annoyed because I think I'm better than meets expectations but the things I'm good at aren't the things they measure. They don't have a box for "made a scared kid feel safe enough to read out loud." They have a box for "implements behaviour management strategies consistently." I do implement them. I just don't enjoy it and apparently that shows.

Had dinner with Camille and we split a bottle of wine and she told me about some drama at her office and it was exactly what I needed. Not to think about my own stuff for an hour. Good food, good company, clean kitchen afterwards. The week is done.

Nudge: "Is 'meets expectations' actually fine with you?"

Response: No. But what am I going to do, argue with the rubric? I know what they want and I know I could give it to them if I tried harder. I just don't want to become that teacher. The one the kids go quiet around. I'd rather meet expectations and keep the classroom feeling like a place they actually want to be.

Had a meeting with Dr. Villanueva about the dissertation chapter on extractive policy in Peru. For once he actually engaged with the argument instead of just redirecting me toward his framework. He pushed back on my case selection, which was fair, but he did it by asking questions rather than just telling me to change it. That felt different. I left his office thinking clearly for the first time in weeks.

Walked to the mercado afterward and sat with a coffee going through my notes. The chapter outline is solid. The theoretical framing is mine -- not his, not borrowed from the lab's usual line. I've been circling this argument for months and it finally has shape. There's a version of this that could become a standalone article, which matters because I need publications that aren't just appendages to his projects.

I forget sometimes that I came here with a decade of professional experience. I wasn't some kid who wandered into a program. I chose this. I left a salary and a title because the work I was doing in Bogota hit a ceiling I couldn't break through without the credential. Days like today remind me the trade-off has a point.

Nudge: "That chapter outline is yours -- what usually pulls you away from it?"

Lab meeting today was three hours of performative agreement. Villanueva presented his new grant proposal and everyone nodded along like it was revelation. Half the methodology section is recycled from a paper we all know didn't replicate well. I sat there biting my tongue because the last time I raised a concern in front of the group he reframed it as me "not understanding the nuance yet." In front of the first-years. That was six months ago and I still feel it.

Marina, the other senior student, caught my eye during his presentation. She sees it too but she's smarter about it than I am -- she just stays quiet, publishes on the side, and keeps her head down. I can't do that. Something in me won't let me just sit there when the reasoning is sloppy. But speaking up costs more than staying silent, at least in this lab.

Came home and opened the Peru chapter again. The contrast is sharp. In Villanueva's lab I'm a student who should listen. In my own work I'm building something that could actually matter for how these countries manage their mining conflicts. The gap between those two versions of me is getting harder to hold.

Nudge: "What does Marina's approach cost her that yours doesn't?"

Got an email from a former colleague in Bogota asking if I'd co-author a policy brief on lithium regulation for a think tank. Good money, my name on it, and the topic overlaps with my dissertation. I said yes immediately. Then spent the rest of the afternoon wondering if I should have checked with Villanueva first. The program technically doesn't require it, but there's an unspoken expectation that outside work gets cleared through him.

The thing that bothers me is the hesitation itself. I spent eight years writing policy documents without asking anyone's permission. Now I'm second-guessing a decision I'm fully qualified to make because someone with a title might disapprove. I don't know when I started treating his opinion as a gate I have to pass through, but I notice it more and more.

Same day, later. Called my mother in Cali to tell her about the policy brief. She was happy, asked how much it paid, then reminded me that my cousin Luis just got promoted to director at the ministry. She doesn't mean it as a comparison but it lands like one. Luis is 33, has a corner office, and never left the country. I'm 38, sharing a desk in a research lab.

But then she said something that settled me: "You always needed to understand why things work, not just that they work." She's right. That's why consulting felt hollow toward the end. I was writing recommendations I didn't fully believe in because the analysis was never deep enough. The PhD is slow and humbling but at least I'm doing the actual thinking now.

Nudge: "Is the thinking deeper now, or just differently constrained?"

Response: Different constraints, sure. But at least I chose these ones.

Villanueva told me today that he wants to be listed as co-author on the Peru chapter if I submit it to a journal. His reasoning is that the "intellectual environment of the lab" contributed to the work. I pushed back. Told him the chapter uses a different framework than anything the lab has produced, that the fieldwork contacts are mine from my Bogota years, that the data collection was funded by my own assistantship hours. He got quiet in that way he does -- not angry, just recalibrating.

He said we'd "revisit it later," which means he expects me to come around. I won't. This is the one piece of work in this entire program that is entirely mine. Every argument, every source, every late night rewriting the methodology section because his suggestions didn't fit. I'm not handing that over.

I know how this looks from outside. Grad student refusing to share credit with advisor. But this isn't about generosity. If I publish this with his name on it, it becomes part of his portfolio, his grant narrative, his legacy. My contribution disappears into his. I've watched it happen to Marina three times already. That's not going to be me.

Nudge: "What happens if he doesn't let it go?"

Response: I'll figure that out when it comes. Right now the chapter is mine.

Can't focus. Keep replaying the conversation with Villanueva. The way he leaned back in his chair. The pause before he said "revisit." I know that move because I used to do it in meetings in Bogota -- the deliberate silence that says I'm not done with this. He's not going to drop it. And the problem is he doesn't need to fight me directly. He just needs to delay things. Slow down feedback on my other chapters. Forget to sign off on conference funding. The machinery of a program has a hundred small levers.

Started the policy brief for the Bogota think tank. The writing came fast, like muscle memory. Two thousand words in three hours. It reminded me of what it feels like to work without someone standing over the output, deciding if it's good enough to carry their name.

Had lunch with Marina. She told me Villanueva asked her opinion about my "collaboration style." That's how he phrases it -- not that I'm difficult, just that my style needs adjustment. She told him I'm thorough. Diplomatic of her. We both know what he's doing. At least someone in that lab sees it clearly.

Nudge: "What would it change if you and Marina pushed back together?"

Later in the evening. Finished the first draft of the policy brief. Sent it to the think tank contact. Fourteen pages, clean argument, clear recommendations. Took me less than a week of evening work. The dissertation chapter, by contrast, has been in revision for four months because every piece of feedback from Villanueva sends me in a different direction.

Thinking about what Marina said at lunch, about his use of "collaboration style." It's a framing that puts the problem on me, on how I work, instead of on the fact that he's claiming credit for something he didn't build. Language like that is how people with institutional standing rewrite the narrative. I've seen it in government -- the person with the title gets to define the terms.

I need to decide soon whether to submit the Peru chapter independently or wait for whatever "revisit" means. Waiting gives him time to build his case or create friction. Submitting means the relationship might not recover. My mother would say "no pelees batallas que no puedes ganar." But I'm not sure this is a battle I can afford to lose either. The chapter is the best thing I've written. If it goes out with his name, the work is still good. But it won't be mine anymore.

Nudge: "Which loss would sit with you longer?"

Spent the whole morning at Leopold's working on sketches for the cafe mural commission. There's something about sitting in a busy space that helps ideas flow — all that ambient noise and people-watching. I ordered three cups of chai and didn't feel guilty about it. The sketches are coming together. Abstract florals with these hidden eyes peeking through petals. The cafe owner wanted "something colorful" which honestly gives me so much room to play.

I keep thinking about what Neha said last week, how she can't understand spending money on experiences when we're always behind on rent. But like... what's the point of hustling if you never actually enjoy anything? I'm not being irresponsible. I finished the commission brief. I just also let myself have a nice morning. Both things can be true.

Ma called again. The usual script. "Beta, you're 22 now. Meera aunty's daughter just got placed at Infosys. When are you going to think about your future?" I tried to explain that I AM thinking about my future, that the mural commission could lead to more work with the cafe chain, that I've been approached about teaching at an actual studio. But she doesn't hear any of that. She only hears "no salary, no benefits, no husband."

I hung up crying. Not because I doubt myself — I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do. But it's exhausting to constantly defend my choices to people who refuse to see them as valid. Papa at least stays quiet now. But Ma... she makes it sound like I'm throwing my life away for finger painting.

Rohan came over with biriyani and let me rant for an hour. He gets it — his family was the same way until he started making decent money from photography. He said something that stuck with me: "They'll come around when they see you're serious. You just have to outlast their doubt." But how long is that supposed to take? And why should I have to prove myself to people who should just... believe in me?

I painted until 3am. Angry brushstrokes that somehow turned into something beautiful. Maybe that's the only way I know how to process this.

Nudge: "That sounds really heavy — the gap between how you see your path and how your Ma sees it. What would it take for you to feel at peace with that gap, even if she never comes around?"

Response: Honestly? I don't know if I can feel at peace with it. Part of me still wants her approval even though I hate admitting that.

Teaching day. Seven kids between ages 8-12, all hyped up on whatever sugar their parents gave them before drop-off. We did texture collages — fabric scraps, sandpaper, dried leaves. One kid, Advait, made this incredible piece with torn newspaper headlines layered under tissue paper. He said it was "all the boring words adults say, trapped under clouds." Eight years old. I was floored.

Sometimes I think about what I would've been like if someone had let me make weird art at that age instead of drilling multiplication tables into my skull. Maybe I'd be further along now. Maybe not. Hard to know. Anyway the studio paid me in cash and I bought myself fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not, the day was good.

Finished the Leopold mural. Owner loved it. He's already talking about his next location.

Nudge: "That's a big milestone — completing a commission and getting repeat interest. How did it feel standing back and looking at the finished piece?"

Response: Like I could breathe for the first time in weeks. That moment when you step back and it actually looks like what you imagined... there's nothing like it.

Ran into Vikram uncle at the market today — Papa's old colleague. He made this comment about how I should "use my artistic talents for graphic design, something practical." As if he knows anything about my career. As if I haven't already considered every possible path and deliberately chosen this one.

I know people mean well. I know they're just parroting the same fears their parents parroted to them. But I'm so tired of being treated like I haven't thought this through. I've thought about it more than any of them ever thought about their own safe little corporate jobs. I made a CHOICE. Why is that so hard to respect?

Nudge: "You've had a few encounters lately where you've had to defend your path. Do you notice this affecting how you feel about your work day-to-day, or does it stay separate?"
Three workshops back to back this weekend. Good money but I'm running on fumes. Need to protect my energy better.

Had a rare quiet day. Slept in until noon, made myself a proper breakfast instead of grabbing biscuits, then just... existed. Watered all the plants. Put on a Norah Jones record. Sat by the window with my sketchbook and didn't force anything. Some days the best thing I can do is nothing at all.

I've been thinking about what success means to me. Society has this very narrow definition — salary, title, marriage, house. But when I imagine my ideal life, it's this: a studio of my own, enough commissions to stay comfortable, mornings where I can drink chai without rushing, the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me. That's it. That's the whole dream.

People act like wanting to enjoy your life is lazy or lacking drive. But I think it takes more courage to opt out of the rat race than to join it. Anyone can chase money. Not everyone can figure out what actually makes them happy and then structure their life around it.

The mural money came through. I'm putting half toward rent and supplies, and the other half toward that weekend trip to Goa with Rohan and Meera. Non-negotiable. We need this.

Nudge: "That vision of success — studio, comfortable enough, mornings without rushing. What would have to change for that to feel within reach rather than aspirational?"

Response: Honestly it doesn't feel that far off. A few more consistent clients, maybe. Right now I'm just focused on the Goa trip.

Back from Goa. Sand in places sand should not be. Sunburn on my shoulders. Zero regrets. We found this tiny beach shack that served the best fish curry I've ever had and we went back three nights in a row. Meera finally stopped stressing about her startup for five minutes. Rohan got some incredible sunset shots. I filled half a sketchbook with quick studies — fishing boats, palm shadows, the way light hits water.

This is what I work for. Not to accumulate stuff. Just to have these moments. To sit on a beach with people I love and think "this is exactly where I want to be." The work will always be there when I get back. But time with the people who matter, doing things that feel good — that's the whole point.

Can't stop thinking about today. There was this older man, Mr. Hernandez, and he reminded me so much of my tio — the way he called me "mija" and told me about his grandchildren back in Honduras. He needed his blood pressure medication but couldn't afford the copay, kept apologizing like he was asking for something unreasonable. And I just... I knew we have samples. I know where they are. I could have grabbed a month's supply and no one would have noticed or cared.

But I didn't. Followed protocol. Gave him the paperwork for financial assistance that takes six weeks to process. He thanked me anyway, squeezed my hand, said God would bless me for my kindness. Kindness. I filled out a form.

The rules exist for a reason, right? That's what my supervisor says. That's what I tell myself when I'm lying in bed at 3am staring at the ceiling instead of sleeping. The system works when everyone follows it. My abuela used to say you earn respect by doing things the right way, even when the right way is hard. She volunteered here for decades and never took a single bandaid home that wasn't accounted for.

But also — what is the system actually doing for Mr. Hernandez? He's 68, worked construction his whole life, and now he can't afford pills that cost the hospital pennies. I went into nursing because I wanted to help people. Real help. Not paperwork.

I keep telling myself I'll feel better about this tomorrow. I won't.

Nudge: "What would your abuela have done?"

Response: She would have followed the rules. She always did. But things were different then — there wasn't so much bureaucracy, people looked out for each other more. Anyway, it doesn't matter. I have to get ready for my shift.

Told my mom I can't come home for Christmas this year because of work. It's not entirely a lie — I could have requested off earlier. Just didn't.

Nudge: "Why didn't you?"

Response: Because part of me wanted an excuse. Everyone will be there — tias, primos, the whole family asking when I'm getting married, why I'm not going to church anymore, if I'm eating enough. I love them but I come back feeling like I'm failing at being who they expect me to be. At least at the hospital I know what I'm supposed to do.

Good shift today. We had a Guatemalan family come in — the grandmother only spoke K'iche' and was terrified. I don't speak it fluently but I know enough from my own abuela to say the important things: you're safe, we're going to help you, your family is right outside.

She grabbed my hand during the IV and prayed. I prayed with her, quietly, in Spanish. Felt right. The daughter cried when I came out to update them. Said they'd never been treated with such dignity at a hospital before. That's the job. That's why I'm here.

I hate the new scheduling software. It's supposed to be "fair" and "optimized" but it has me working Christmas Eve AND Christmas Day, back to back twelve hour shifts. Meanwhile Sarah who has no family obligations gets the whole week off because she requested it first. Cool. Great. Love that for her.

Called my mom to complain and she just said "Ay mija, that's how it is. At least you have a good job." Which I know is true. I know I'm lucky. I know people would kill for this position. But I'm allowed to be annoyed, right? I haven't had a Christmas off in three years. Three years of sending gifts instead of being there. Three years of video calls while everyone eats together.

Nudge: "What's the hardest part of not being there?"

Response: Watching them on the screen and knowing they're making memories without me. My little cousin is seven now and she barely knows who I am. Last year she asked my mom if I was the one who sends the presents. Not tia Valentina. Just "the one who sends presents." I'm becoming a stranger to my own family and I can't do anything about it because someone has to cover the holiday shifts and I'm the one without kids.

Worked sixteen hours. A man died on my floor — no family, no one to call. I held his hand at the end because no one should die alone. My family sent me a video of them opening presents and I couldn't watch it.

Nudge: "You could be there for him but not for them?"

Everyone's going out tonight. I'm eating leftover tamales my neighbor brought me and going to bed early. Told myself I'd call my mom at midnight her time but I'll probably be asleep. Another year of choosing this life over the one I was supposed to have.

Nudge: "What life were you supposed to have?"

Response: Married by now, probably. Living close to home, seeing my family every Sunday, raising kids who speak Spanish as their first language and know how to make tamales and go to mass. That's what my mom did, what her mom did. Instead I'm alone in an apartment eating someone else's cooking and pretending this is enough because I'm "making a difference." I don't know if I'm brave for building something new or just selfish for leaving everything behind.

The new postdoc presented my stakeholder mapping framework at the department seminar today. Used my slides, my structure, even the same case example I developed last spring. Introduced it as "something I've been working on." I sat there and said nothing. I don't know why I didn't say anything.

Nudge: "What stopped you?"

Long day in the library. Finished the literature review chapter draft, or at least a version of it. Grabbed a coffee at Fika with Astrid from the history department, talked about nothing in particular. Sometimes those conversations are the best part of the week.

Nudge: "What makes those the best part?"

Response: Nobody's positioning themselves. Nobody's measuring what you've published or where your funding comes from. She just talks about medieval trade routes and I talk about regulatory capture and we both complain about the printing queue. It's uncomplicated. Most things here aren't.

Supervisory meeting ran long. Björn wants me to restructure chapter three around a different theoretical lens. I think the existing framing is stronger, but he has a point about the reviewers at the journal we're targeting. Sometimes you have to write for the audience that matters.

Spent the evening trying to reorganize the argument. My eyes are burning. The flat is a mess. Haven't cooked in three days, just bread and cheese and whatever's in the fridge. I keep telling myself the pace will ease up after the mid-term review, but I said the same thing last semester.

Couldn't sleep. Kept turning over the chapter three issue. It's not really about the theoretical lens, if I'm honest. Björn's framing would position his own work more centrally in my literature review. He didn't say that outright, but it's obvious when you look at the citations he suggested. Three of the five are his papers.

This is the game though. Your supervisor's name opens doors. If citing him more prominently gets me a better review at the European Journal of Political Economy, fine. The work is still mine. The data is mine. The analysis is mine. A few extra citations don't change the substance, even if they change the optics.

I ran into Erik from the cohort below mine in the corridor earlier. He asked about the committee restructuring vote next week. He's campaigning for a seat. I told him what I knew, which wasn't everything. You learn not to give away the full picture when people are vying for the same limited spots. That sounds worse written down than it felt in the moment.

The departmental retreat was today. Eight hours in a conference room discussing the new research strategy. The head of department asked for input from doctoral students, which in practice means she asked, two of us spoke, and then the senior faculty made the decisions they were always going to make.

I'd prepared a brief proposal about reallocating seminar slots to give doctoral candidates more presenting time. Wrote it up properly, referenced comparable departments, included a simple implementation plan. Professor Ekström listened, nodded, said "interesting idea" and moved to the next agenda item. That was it. Twenty minutes of preparation, ten seconds of attention.

What gets me is that I know how these rooms work. I spent years in rooms like this in the private sector. The difference is that in consulting, if you brought a proposal with data, people engaged with it. Here, the hierarchy is softer but somehow more rigid. Nobody tells you no. They just don't pick it up. The proposal sits there and eventually everyone forgets it existed. Including, probably, me.

Nudge: "That sounds like a lot of effort for ten seconds."

Response: It was. And the worst part is I'll do it again. I'll prepare something else, present it calmly, and watch it dissolve. Because the alternative is not trying, and I can't do that. If I stop pushing, I'm just another doctoral student waiting for permission. I've been someone who makes things happen for too long to accept that. But I'm starting to wonder if making things happen here requires a different kind of leverage than I'm used to.

Applied for that visiting researcher slot at Sciences Po. Had everything ready, CV updated, research statement polished, a solid letter from Björn. Then the department administrator told me the application needs a sign-off from the graduate studies committee, and they don't meet until November. The deadline is October 15th. She said I could email the committee chair directly to request an exception but "couldn't guarantee anything."

So now I'm just waiting. I emailed the chair four days ago, very polite, laid out the timeline clearly. No response. I don't even know if she's read it. This is what drives me up the wall, being stuck because someone else hasn't gotten around to clicking reply. I could have the whole thing sorted in an afternoon if it were up to me.

Nudge: "What would you do if you knew the answer was no?"

Response: I'd find another route. There's always another route. Maybe I'd contact Sciences Po directly, ask if they'd accept a provisional approval. Or find a different programme with a later deadline. Standing still isn't really something I do well.

Astrid asked me today, very casually over lunch, why I "always need to be in charge of things." She was half joking but it landed. I told her I don't need to be in charge, I just don't like waiting for other people to do things badly.

She laughed it off. I didn't.

The thing is, people say that like it's a character flaw. Like wanting things to go well and being willing to do the work to make that happen is somehow suspect. When I chaired the doctoral council meeting last week, I'd read all the documents beforehand. I'd drafted an agenda. I'd emailed people about their action items from the previous meeting. Is that controlling, or is that just doing the job properly? Nobody complained when the meeting finished in forty minutes instead of the usual hour and a half.

I know I come on strong sometimes. I know I take up more space than the average doctoral student. But I came here after running a team of twelve. I'm not going to pretend I don't know how to organise people or push things forward. If that makes someone uncomfortable, I think that says more about their expectations of what a doctoral student should be than about me.

Meeting with Björn and the co-supervisor today about the corporate partnership deliverables. I had a clear view on which case studies to prioritise for the industry report. Björn agreed. The co-supervisor, Lena, pushed for a different set entirely. Her reasoning was that the cases I chose were too "practitioner-oriented" and wouldn't serve the academic paper well enough.

I pushed back a little but she was firm, and Björn stayed quiet, which effectively meant she won. So we're going with her cases. I can already see the gaps. The data is thinner, the industry partner will be less interested, and I'll be the one fielding their questions about why the report doesn't address what they actually care about. But sure. Her cases it is.

End of the first proper week back after the holiday break and I am completely drained. The kids were wild, which I expected, but what wore me out more was this long staff meeting about the new curriculum rollout. Lots of talk about standardized testing targets and almost nothing about whether the material actually reflects what is happening in the world right now.

I brought up the idea of including more content on different cultures since half our class comes from families that moved here within the last decade. My mentor teacher said it was "a nice thought" and then immediately moved on to the testing calendar. Nobody else said anything. I sat through the rest of it feeling like I had spoken out of turn somehow.

Came home and Sofie had made soup, which was kind. Ate it on the couch and fell asleep before nine. Not sure I even processed the day properly, just ran out of battery entirely.

Nudge: "What made you bring it up in the first place?"

Response: Half the kids in my class have parents who came from Syria or Turkey or Eritrea. The textbooks barely mention any of those places. It felt dishonest not to say something. I just wish I had been less surprised when nobody backed me up.

Can't stop thinking about this article I read before bed about microplastics found in baby formula. Baby formula. These are infants who did not ask for any of this and the regulatory bodies are apparently just watching it happen. I teach six-year-olds. Some of them were drinking that formula two years ago.

I mentioned it to Sofie and she said "you can't carry all of this" which I know is probably true but also feels like giving up. What am I supposed to do, just not read about it? Pretend it is not happening? I teach children. Their health is literally part of my job even if no one puts it in the job description.

I know I am spiraling a bit. I get like this when something hits close to the work. It is not even the microplastics specifically, it is this feeling that the systems that are supposed to protect people just don't, and we all keep going to work the next morning like that is fine.

Volunteered at the community center after school. Helped a girl named Amira with her reading homework and she finished a whole chapter book for the first time. Walked home in the cold and felt like it was a good day.

Nudge: "What was it like watching Amira finish that book?"

Response: She looked so shocked at herself. Like she did not believe she could do it. I told her she did and she just sat there grinning with the book in her lap. Moments like that remind me why I got into this. It is small, one kid, one book, but it felt real in a way that staff meetings never do.

Had dinner with my parents in Aalst this evening. My dad works at a chemical plant and he was complaining about new emission regulations making his shifts more complicated. I wanted to say that the regulations exist for a reason, that the Scheldt has been polluted for decades and people downstream are still dealing with it. But I just nodded and ate my stoofvlees.

It is strange because I love my dad and I know he is tired and just wants his job to be manageable.
