But I also know that the river matters and the people who fish in it matter and the kids who swim in it matter. I do not know how to hold both of those things at the same time without feeling like I am betraying one side.

This keeps happening. At school, at home, in my own head. I see the bigger picture and then I see the person in front of me and I cannot figure out how to be honest without being hurtful. So I stay quiet, which feels like its own kind of failure.

Nudge: "When you stay quiet, who are you protecting?"

Response: My dad, mostly. Or the version of our relationship where we do not argue about things that matter. But I think I am also protecting myself from having to admit that I do not know how to change anyone's mind. If I say something and he dismisses it, then I have to sit with the fact that caring about this stuff does not actually move anything. At least silence lets me keep the illusion that I could make a difference if I tried.

Sofie asked me tonight if I ever take a break from "worrying about the world." She said it like she was being kind but it stung. I am not worrying for fun. I am paying attention, which apparently is unusual enough that people think you need a hobby.

I told her I have hobbies. I run. I cook. She said those do not count because I listen to podcasts about ocean acidification while I run and I only buy from the organic market even when it is out of the way. I did not have a great comeback for that.

Spent the afternoon at the community center again, this time sorting donated winter coats. The pile was massive, people just brought bags and bags. Some of the coats still had tags on them. I paired up with this older woman named Fatima who has been volunteering there for eleven years and she told me stories about the first families she helped resettle.

She said something that stuck. She said you do not help because it fixes everything, you help because the person in front of you is cold. Simple as that. I think I needed to hear it from someone who has been doing this longer than I have been alive.

Walked home through the park afterward and the light was doing that thing where everything looks like a painting. Bare trees, pink sky. I felt calm for the first time in a while. Not because anything got solved, but because I spent the day doing something that made direct, visible sense. Coat goes to person. Person is warm. Done.

Good week at school somehow. I have been trying this thing where I let the kids ask questions about anything during the last ten minutes of Friday and we just talk about it. One kid asked why some people do not have houses. Another asked if fish can feel pain. I did not have neat answers but we talked through them together and I think that matters more anyway.

My mentor teacher actually came by during one of these sessions and did not say anything negative afterward, which for her is practically applause. She said "they were engaged" and left. I will take it.

After school I sat in the classroom for a while marking worksheets and I had this rare moment of thinking I am in the right place. Not that everything is fine, obviously, the world is a mess and the school system has problems and I am twenty-two and exhausted most of the time. But the work itself, sitting with a room of six-year-olds and trying to help them make sense of things, that part is right.

Nudge: "What changed this week compared to those first weeks back?"

Response: I think I stopped waiting for permission. The curriculum rollout meeting frustrated me because I was asking the institution to make space for what I cared about. This week I just did it myself, ten minutes, no approval needed. It is small but it reminded me that I do not have to fix the whole system to do something meaningful inside it. Fatima said the same thing in her own way. Maybe it is finally sinking in.

Got into it with a colleague at lunch. She was talking about how the school should not "get political" by including climate content in the science module for the older kids. I said the climate is not political, it is physical reality, and she said I was being naive. I told her that ignoring it is a choice too and then the conversation got awkward and we both went back to eating.

I know I was right but I also know I was sharp about it. My hands were shaking a bit after, which always happens when I get confrontational. I hate that about myself. I cannot tell if the shaking is because I am angry or because I am scared of what people think of me when I push back. Probably both.

Nudge: "Would you say the same thing again tomorrow?"

Response: Yes. But maybe less sharply. She is not the enemy, she is just someone who has not thought about it the way I have. Or she has and arrived somewhere different, which is harder to accept. Either way, I do not want to be the person who stays quiet when it matters. I already do that enough at home.

Nothing really happened today worth writing about. Spent most of the morning in back-to-back syncs, the usual Monday chaos of catching up on what slipped over the weekend. Ate lunch at my desk again. Kimchi jjigae from the place downstairs, same order for probably the third week running. I keep meaning to try something different but I'm always too rushed to look at the menu properly.

Had a longer-than-expected call with our warehouse partner in Da Nang about the delayed shipment of syringes. Turned out to be a customs paperwork issue on their end. Nothing dramatic. I walked them through it, helped them resubmit. Could've passed it off to my team but I know that particular contact better and it just went faster to do it myself.

After work I sat on the bus home and watched people through the window for a while. Everyone with their faces in their phones. Some guy was asleep with his mouth open, briefcase sliding off his lap. I thought about how we all just do this every day, the same routes, the same tiredness, and nobody really sees each other. Not a depressing thought exactly, just a passing one. Cooked ramyeon at home, watched half an episode of something, fell asleep on the couch.

Nudge: "Why'd you handle the Da Nang call yourself instead of passing it off?"

Response: Honestly, Minh's been dealing with enough on his end. Felt wrong to make him explain the same issue to someone new on my team. It was just easier and faster.

Team meeting today, Director Kwon floated cutting two more rural routes because margins are too thin. I just sat there and nodded. Didn't say a word.

Nudge: "What stopped you from pushing back?"

Response: I don't know. Wasn't the right moment, maybe.

I keep circling back to something from this week. We had to choose between two packaging suppliers for the clinic kits. One is cheaper, faster turnaround, but their labor practices are questionable -- there were reports last year about conditions at their factories in Guangdong. The other costs about twelve percent more and would push our Q2 numbers into uncomfortable territory. I went with the cheaper one.

I told myself it was the responsible decision for the company. That keeping costs down means we can serve more routes, reach more clinics. And that's true, on paper. But I also know part of it is that I didn't want to have the fight with Kwon about the budget variance. The cheaper option was the path of least resistance, and I took it.

My mom would say I'm overthinking it. She'd say, "You can't fix everything, just do your job well." And she's probably right in a practical sense. But I keep seeing the article I read about those factory conditions. Young workers, long shifts, dormitory housing. I approved the PO this morning and then went to get coffee and stood in front of the machine for a full minute just staring at the buttons. I don't know what that was about.

Dinner with some old consulting friends on Saturday. One of them, Sungmin, was going on about this new client of his, some fast fashion brand expanding into Vietnam. He was bragging about the margins they're projecting and how they found manufacturers who'd undercut everyone. I sat there eating my samgyeopsal and just let him talk. Didn't say anything. Didn't even raise an eyebrow.

It bothered me all weekend. Not because of Sungmin -- he's always been like that, it's his whole thing. What bothered me is I used to be the one who'd at least say something. Even a joke, something to signal that I thought it was messed up. This time I just chewed my food and changed the subject to football. I don't know when I became the guy who lets it slide.

Nudge: "When did you stop being that guy?"

Response: Maybe I never really was. Maybe I just liked thinking of myself that way. Easier to make a comment at dinner than to actually do something about it at work.
Spent Saturday morning with the cleanup crew along the river near Yeouido. Pulled out a shopping cart and about thirty plastic bags from one stretch. Didn't think about work the entire time.

I've been thinking about the annual vendor review coming up next month and I already know I'm going to let the Guangdong supplier pass without flagging the labor stuff. It's not that I don't care. I do. But raising it means a formal review process, means Kwon gets involved, means I have to justify the cost difference all over again, and last time I brought up anything remotely like this he looked at me like I was wasting his time. So I'll probably just sign off on the renewal and move on.

The thing is, I know exactly what the right thing to do is. Or at least what I think is right. It's not even ambiguous. But the gap between knowing and doing keeps getting wider and I can feel it. Like there's a version of me that would've already drafted the memo and cc'd everyone and not cared about the politics. And then there's the version of me that's sitting here at midnight writing about it in a journal instead.

Talked to my younger sister Eunji on the phone tonight. She's finishing her master's thesis on microplastics in Korean coastal waters. She was excited about some data she found. I listened and nodded and felt something like envy, which is a strange thing to feel toward your own sister. She just seems so clear about what matters to her and what she's willing to do about it.

Nudge: "What is it about Eunji that you're actually envying?"

Sunday. Woke up late, made doenjang-guk with leftover vegetables. Sat by the window eating and watching the pigeons on the building across the street. One of them kept trying to land on a ledge that was too narrow and sliding off, over and over. Kept at it though.

Went grocery shopping after. The ajumma at the market stall I always go to asked about my mom, which she does every time. I buy too many spring onions from her because I can never say no when she bundles in extras. Walked around Mapo for a while after, no real destination. Passed by the old bookstore on the corner and picked up a used copy of a Murakami novel I've already read twice. Something about the cover design on this edition was different and I liked it.

Called my mom in the evening. She was watching a cooking competition show and narrating the whole thing to me like I could see it. She does this. I mostly just listened. She asked if I'm eating properly and sleeping enough, the standard questions. I told her yes to both, which is half true. She told me my cousin Hyunwoo got promoted at his bank job and I said that's great. She didn't compare us, she never does, but I could hear her thinking about it. Or maybe that's just me projecting. Hard to tell sometimes.

Nudge: "Is the comparison coming from her or from you?"

Got the quarterly report today and the numbers tell a clear story. The rural routes we kept are operating at a loss, just like Kwon said they would. And the two routes he cut last month? The clinics on those routes are now getting supplies every six weeks instead of every two. I found that out from a field coordinator in the Philippines who emailed me directly asking what happened. I didn't have a good answer for her. I just said we were "restructuring delivery schedules" which is corporate for "we stopped caring."

I drafted a response about four times and deleted each one. The honest version would've been something like: I disagreed with this decision and didn't fight hard enough to stop it. But you can't send that in a work email. So I sent the sanitized version and closed my laptop and went for a walk.

Ended up at the river, not for cleanup, just to sit. It was cold and there weren't many people around. I watched the water for a while and thought about what Eunji said last week about her research -- how she said even small data points matter because they add up over time into something you can't ignore. I keep thinking about that in the context of my own work. Every route we cut, every supplier I don't flag, every meeting where I stay quiet -- those are data points too. They add up into something. I just haven't decided yet what that something is, or what I'm going to do about it.

Spent the entire afternoon at the community center helping Fatima with her citizenship application paperwork. Her English is getting so much better but the legal language still trips her up, and honestly it trips me up too. We went through every section three times to make sure nothing would get flagged. By the end my eyes were burning from staring at that tiny print.

But there was this moment when we finished and she grabbed my hands and just held them for a second without saying anything. Eight months ago she arrived here with two suitcases and three kids and now she's applying to become a citizen. I drove home feeling like maybe I actually did something that mattered today. The dishes were still in the sink and Mia needed help with her history project but for once I didn't feel bad about where my time went.

Nudge: "What usually makes a day feel like it didn't matter?"

Response: The days blur together sometimes. Errands, laundry, driving kids places. I know it matters, I know keeping a household running matters. But when I lie down at night after a day like that, I can't point to anything and say this moved the needle. This made something better. It's hard to explain without sounding ungrateful for the life I have.

Conference at Mia's school tonight about the AP Environmental Science curriculum and I'm still replaying this one exchange in my head. Another parent, Deborah something, stood up and said the class was spending too much time on "doom and gloom climate stuff" and that her son was coming home anxious. And I wanted to say something, I had the words right there, but I just sat there.

The teacher looked uncomfortable and sort of backpedaled about how they try to balance the science with "solutions" and "hope." And I thought about Mia last week telling me she doesn't want to have kids because what's the point. And I thought about all the reports I've read. And I said nothing. Just sat there nodding like everyone else while Deborah congratulated herself for protecting her son from being sad about the world actually ending.

I should have spoken up. I teach my kids to speak up. But I was worried about making things awkward, worried about being that parent, worried about the car ride home with Mia being embarrassed. Now I'm sitting here at midnight wishing I could do it over.

Nudge: "What stopped you in that moment?"

Response: I think I was scared of my own anger. If I started talking I wasn't sure I could keep it civil. And there's this voice in my head that says people like Deborah can't be convinced anyway so why bother, why make yourself a target. But that's just an excuse isn't it. The teacher needed backup and I let her down.

Recycling day. Pulled out three bags of perfectly recyclable materials that neighbors just threw in with their regular trash. Aluminum cans, glass jars, stuff that takes thirty seconds to rinse out. I sorted through it all anyway because what else am I going to do, let it go to the landfill?

Tom thinks I take this too seriously. He said as much when I came back inside smelling like someone's old salsa jar. He's not wrong that I can't fix everyone else's habits but he's also not standing in the garage at 7am picking yogurt containers out of a trash bag. Sometimes I wonder if I'm the crazy one, if normal people just throw things away and sleep fine at night and don't think about where it all ends up.

The kids have learned to separate everything correctly, at least. That's something. But then I think about the scale of it, the millions of tons, the plastic islands, and my little neighborhood project feels like putting a band-aid on a hemorrhage. And I keep doing it anyway because what's the alternative.

Nudge: "You said 'what's the alternative.' Do you actually have an answer to that?"

Response: Give up and stop caring, I guess. Which isn't really an alternative at all.

Daniel's soccer game this morning, then dropped Mia at her study group, then grocery run, then made dinner for a family from the resettlement center whose mom just had surgery. Pretty standard Saturday honestly. I'm beat.

Noticed while shopping that they stopped carrying the fair trade coffee I usually get. Asked the manager and he said it wasn't selling enough. Ended up buying the regular kind because I needed coffee and didn't have time to go somewhere else. Small thing but it's been nagging at me all evening.
Mia asked if she could join me at the refugee center next week. Said she wanted to help with the kids' tutoring program. Didn't push her for a reason, just said yes.

My sister called to catch up and somehow we ended up in this whole thing about how I spend my time. She asked when I was going to "go back to real work" since the kids are older now. As if what I do isn't work. As if coordinating thirty households' recycling and showing up for families who have no one else isn't real.

I didn't say any of that obviously. Just said I'm happy with how things are, which is mostly true. But later Tom asked what was wrong and I snapped at him for no reason. I know she doesn't mean it as a criticism, she just doesn't get it. Not everyone measures their life by a paycheck. I'm doing what feels right even if it looks like nothing on a resume.

Nudge: "What part of her question stung the most?"

Response: That she might be right. That maybe I'm hiding behind volunteer work because I'm scared I couldn't cut it in a real job anymore. That eight years out of the workforce, who would even hire me now. Easier to tell myself I'm making a difference here than to test whether I still could somewhere else.

My hands are still shaking from the critique session. Professor Mansour put my naskh panel up on the wall next to everyone else's work and asked the class, "Where is the artist in this?" Like I was just copying. Like three months of grinding ink and cutting reeds and adjusting every single letter weight was nothing. Somebody said it looked like something you'd find in a mosque gift shop. People laughed.

I didn't say anything. I sat there and took it. Walked straight to the bathroom after and just stood there gripping the sink. The worst part is I keep replaying it and I still can't figure out what I should have said. I know they don't get it. But that doesn't make the laughing stop echoing in my head.

Nudge: "What would you have said if you could go back?"

Response: That the script isn't decoration. That every stroke has a genealogy — my jiddo's hand over mine, guiding the qalam for years. That I didn't choose this style from a catalogue. But I don't know if saying that would have changed anything. They'd probably still just see an old alphabet on paper.

Spent the morning helping Teta Hana sort through jiddo's old papers again, she wants to frame some of his practice sheets, the ones with his warmup strokes, hundreds of little alifs in neat rows fading where the ink ran dry. She held one up and just smiled and didn't say anything. Put it aside for me.

Then I go to studio and Layla is laser-cutting plexiglass and Rami's doing something with projectors and sand and I'm sitting there with my inkwell and bamboo and I keep thinking do I look like a museum exhibit to them, like am I doing something real or am I just holding onto something because letting go would feel like — I don't know. I keep not finishing that thought.

Nudge: "Feel like what?"

Response: Like forgetting him. Like those practice sheets stop meaning anything. I know that doesn't make logical sense, I could still remember jiddo without doing calligraphy. But it would feel like I walked away from the table where he taught me and never came back.

Professor Mansour pulled me aside after class and said she sees potential in my work but wants me to "break the script apart." She used words like deconstruct, fragment, subvert. She wants me to take the naskh letters and tear them up, layer them over industrial textures, make them unrecognizable. She said it would be a strong thesis direction.

I told her I'd think about it. Walked home the long way through Gemmayze. Something about the idea makes my stomach tight. Not because it's technically hard. I could do it. I know how to cut letterforms apart and reassemble them. It's that I keep picturing jiddo watching me do it. And Teta seeing the result on a gallery wall and not being able to read a single word. I said I'd think about it and I will, but the thinking itself feels wrong.

Everyone keeps acting like I'm afraid of experimenting. I'm not afraid. I just don't think ripping something apart is the only way to prove you understand it.

Nudge: "What would proving it look like for you?"

Had dinner at Uncle Fadi's last night and my cousin Reem asked about my thesis. I said calligraphy and she nodded like that was obvious, like of course Nadia's doing that, and I wanted to snap at her. I don't know why. She wasn't being mean. She was being supportive. But the way she said "of course" made me feel like a fixed point everyone's already mapped. Like no one wonders what I'd do if I chose freely.

Then mama brought up jiddo and told the story about him lettering the sign for the pharmacy on Rue Hamra, how the owner cried when he saw it. Everyone smiled. I smiled too. It's a good story. I love that story.

But I sat there holding my plate thinking about Professor Mansour's suggestion and how if I told this table I was going to fragment the script and collage it over concrete textures, my grandmother would go quiet in that particular way she does when she's hurt but won't say so. And my mother would change the subject. And I'd sit there knowing I made a room full of people uncomfortable for the sake of a grade and a gallery slot. So I won't do it. But I keep circling back to the fact that I want to, or at least part of me does, and I can't tell if that part is ambition or betrayal.

Nudge: "You said part of you wants to — which part?"

Response: I don't know. Can we not do this right now.

I did something small today that I keep thinking about. I was in the studio early, nobody else there yet, and I pulled out a fresh sheet and started writing the bismillah in naskh the way jiddo taught me. Perfect form, clean lines. Then without really deciding to, I took a second sheet and wrote it again but let the letters drift apart, stretched the connections, let the lam curve wider than it should. It still read as the phrase. Just looser.

I stared at both sheets for a long time. The second one wasn't ugly. It wasn't disrespectful. It was the same words with more air in them. Teta could still read it. Jiddo would have recognized the hand.

I don't know what this means for my thesis. I rolled both sheets up and put them in my bag. I haven't shown anyone. Part of me thinks if I show Professor Mansour she'll want me to go further, push harder, and I'll end up somewhere I didn't agree to. And part of me thinks if I show Teta she'll look at the second sheet and ask why I'm being careless. So for now they're mine. Two versions of the same prayer, sitting in my bag, and I'm the only one who knows about both.

Long day. There was a curriculum planning session this afternoon and I'd spent most of last weekend drafting a proposal for how we should restructure the Year 9 field trip programme. I'd mapped out logistics, costs, student learning outcomes - the whole thing. I started presenting it and maybe two minutes in, Henrik just cut straight across me. Didn't even raise his hand or wait for a pause. Just started talking about his own idea for partnering with some outdoor centre his friend runs.

And then everyone just... shifted attention to him. Elsa started asking him follow-up questions. The deputy head nodded along. My slides were still up on the projector and nobody even glanced at them. I sat there holding my notes feeling like I'd been physically pushed to the side of the room. I tried to redirect once - said something like "if we could go back to the cost comparison I'd started" - but Henrik just kept going, and the deputy head said we'd "circle back" to mine later. We didn't.

I know I should have pushed harder. I know I should have said "I wasn't finished." But something about the way everyone just moved on made me feel like if I insisted, I'd look petty or desperate. And that felt worse than being ignored. So I just sat there, pretending to take notes, while Henrik got credit for a half-baked version of what I'd already done better. I'm still angry about it and I don't know what to do with that.

Nudge: "What felt worse - being overlooked, or the thought of how you'd look pushing back?"

Response: I think it's the second one, honestly. If I'd just been overlooked that's annoying but I can deal with it. It was the calculation in my head - like, if I fight for this, will they see me as someone with conviction or someone who can't handle being interrupted? And the fact that I even had to think about it made me feel small. I shouldn't have to audition for basic respect.

Good day actually. Parent-teacher conferences tonight and I had back-to-back sessions for three hours straight. My voice is shot and I'm mainlining tea but I feel kind of... alive? There's something about sitting across from a parent and watching their face shift when I show them exactly where their kid improved, with specific examples, with the rubric breakdowns I put together. One mother said she wished all her daughter's teachers were as organised. Another father asked if I'd be willing to mentor his son through the IB application. I said yes before he even finished the sentence.

I don't know why these nights energise me when they exhaust everyone else. Elsa left after her first block looking like she'd run a marathon. But for me it's like - this is where it matters. This is the part where they see you actually know what you're doing. And when they see that, they trust you. And when they trust you, you can actually change things. I reorganised my class seating charts last week based on learning profiles I built myself and two parents tonight specifically mentioned that their kids are more engaged since the change. That's not nothing. That's me actually making a difference because I put myself in a position to.

Just got home. Feet hurt. Going to mark three essays and then collapse. But yeah. Good day.

Submitted the budget request for the Year 9 field trip two weeks ago. Still haven't heard back. I emailed the deputy head on Wednesday, she said it needs to go through the finance committee. I asked when the committee meets. She said "when they have quorum." So basically I'm just supposed to sit here and wait while my timeline collapses.

I've already done all the work. The costing is done, the risk assessments are done, the parent consent forms are drafted. Everything is ready to go and it's being held up by people who haven't even looked at the proposal yet. I followed up again today and got a one-line reply: "We'll get to it." As if my project is some afterthought they'll squeeze in between tea breaks. It's infuriating. If they'd just give me direct access to the budget approval process I could have this sorted in a day. But no, I have to wait for someone else to decide my work is worth looking at.

Nudge: "Is it the delay that's getting to you, or something else?"

Small win today. The head of department asked me to represent our faculty at the district curriculum review panel next month. It's normally a senior teacher slot and she specifically said she chose me because my documentation is the most thorough. Two colleagues with over a decade more experience got passed over for this. I don't feel bad about that. If anything it confirms what I already suspected - that being meticulous matters more than just being around for a long time.

I've already started preparing my presentation notes even though the panel isn't for three weeks. I want to walk in there and have the most polished submission in the room. If I do this well, the district coordinators will know my name. That opens doors. That's how you go from being "the young geography teacher" to someone people actually listen to.

Just came from a department meeting about the spring assessment schedule. I had a clear plan for staggering the exams but Karin pushed back hard, said it would create too much admin work, and the rest just went with her. I caved because I could feel the room turning, but her plan is worse and they'll see that in April when everything piles up.

Nudge: "You said you 'caved.' What would it have taken to hold your ground?"

New teaching assistant started today. She was assigned to support my Year 8 and Year 9 classes. Within the first hour she rearranged my resource station without asking, told a student a different method for the mapping exercise than the one I'd taught, and then during break she suggested to Elsa that my lesson pacing might be "a bit fast for some learners." Elsa told me this casually like it was helpful feedback.

I've been teaching these classes since September. I built the assessment rubrics, the differentiation frameworks, the whole progression structure. And someone who's been in the building for four hours is already second-guessing my methods to a colleague. I pulled her aside after lunch and explained how my classroom runs. She smiled and said "of course, I'm just here to help." But the damage is done - Elsa now thinks my pacing is a problem. I need to correct that impression before it spreads.

District panel prep is going well. Spent the weekend refining my presentation and I'm confident it's strong. Ran through it with the head of department on Friday and she said it was "exactly the level of detail the panel looks for." She also mentioned she'd put my name forward for a cross-school working group on assessment reform that starts after Easter.

The field trip budget finally got approved too - no changes to my original figures. So all that waiting was for nothing, they just rubber-stamped what I'd submitted weeks ago. Annoying but at least it's done. Year 9 students are already asking about it. One of them told me it's the first time a teacher has organised something like this for their year group. I didn't correct her but it's true - nobody else bothered to put the work in. I did.

Had a good run at the RC meeting tonight. The lobby renovation proposal has been stalling for months because of Uncle Tan and his little coalition of holdouts, but I came prepared this time. Pulled together three contractor quotes, a cost-per-household breakdown, a maintenance projection over ten years. Laid it all out on the whiteboard. Even Mrs. Lim, who has been sitting on the fence, nodded along when I showed the depreciation numbers.

The thing is, nobody else would have done this homework. They would have kept going back and forth at every meeting, complaining about the cracked tiles and the peeling paint, never actually resolving anything. I spent two weekends on those quotes. Called in a favor from a guy I know at a building management firm. That is how things get done — you do the legwork, you control the information, you set the direction.

When the vote came, it was seven to two. Uncle Tan and his friend held out but it did not matter. I kept my voice level, thanked everyone for attending, wrapped up in under ninety minutes. Walked home feeling like myself. Like the version of me that knows how to move a room. It has been a while since I felt that at the office.

Managed to restructure the KPI reporting template my department uses before the quarterly review — simplified it, made the data speak for itself instead of burying it in twelve different tabs the way Daniel's team does. My GM noticed during the meeting and called it "clean work." Not a big deal, just a moment where the right thing clicked into place.

Nudge: "What did 'clean work' from the GM mean to you?"

Response: It meant he still sees the difference between someone who knows how to run operations and someone who hides behind frameworks and buzzwords. That is all.

Daniel presented my regional consolidation idea at the leadership sync this morning. Same structure, same three-phase approach I pitched to him informally two weeks ago over coffee. He did not credit me. I sat there, mouth half open, and just let it happen.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying something in that room?"

Response: Everyone was already nodding. If I spoke up it would have looked petty. Like the older guy clinging to credit. I know how that plays. So I sat there and smiled.

The new procurement system rollout requires sign-off from Group IT before we can configure anything for our department. I submitted the request form nine days ago. Called James in IT twice, emailed once, even copied his manager. Got a reply today saying it is "in the queue" and they will "circle back next week."

Meanwhile my team is stuck running parallel processes on the old system, burning hours on manual reconciliation that should have been automated a month ago. I have done everything I can do. The bottleneck is not mine. But when the GM asks why we are behind on the migration timeline, it will be my name on the status report, not James's. Twenty-three years and I still have to sit and wait for a thirty-year-old IT coordinator to decide when my department gets to function properly.

Had a disagreement with the GM over the warehouse staffing model. I have been running the weekend shift allocation the same way for eight years. It works. Attrition is low, the supervisors know the rotation, the overtime costs are predictable. Then Priscilla from HR comes in with a "flexible rostering" proposal that basically throws the whole structure out and lets staff bid on shifts through an app.

I told the GM this would cause chaos. You cannot run a warehouse floor on preferences. People need structure. You need to know who is where and when, and someone needs to own that decision. He listened, then said let us pilot Priscilla's model for one quarter and review. Just like that. Not even a counter — just "let's try it."

I could have pushed harder. I had the data, the history, the operational knowledge. But Priscilla had a slide deck with engagement scores and a Harvard Business Review citation, and I could feel the room tilting her way. So I said fine. I will support the pilot. Shook her hand after. My supervisor Michelle asked me later if I was okay with it. I told her it is what the GM wants, so we execute. That is the job. But I keep running the numbers in my head and I know this is going to cost us. When it does, I will be the one cleaning it up, not Priscilla.

Sat through a two-hour leadership development workshop today. Mandatory for all senior managers. The facilitator — could not have been older than thirty-five — spent forty minutes explaining stakeholder mapping as if half the room had not been managing stakeholders since before she graduated. She asked me to share "a time when I had to earn buy-in from a resistant stakeholder." I gave a polished answer about the Suzhou warehouse relocation in 2019. She smiled and said "great example of vulnerability."

That is not what it was. It was an example of knowing when to push and when to wait, and reading a room of Chinese factory managers who were not going to respond to some consultant's empathy framework. I did not say that. I just nodded. I am too tired to fight these battles anymore. Every year there is a new person who thinks they can teach me how to do what I have been doing for two decades. And every year I sit in the chair and nod.

Nudge: "You've mentioned nodding along a few times now. What would it cost you to stop?"

Response: It is just a workshop. Not worth making a scene over. You pick your battles.

Quiet weekend. Mei Ling had a catering job Saturday so I was home alone most of the day. Made myself kaya toast and coffee for breakfast, read the Straits Times cover to cover. The downstairs coffeeshop uncle gave me an extra egg when I bought lunch, said I looked tired. I probably do.

Watched the F1 qualifying in the evening. Spent too long on a WhatsApp group chat with my army mates arguing about whether the new Tampines hawker centre is better than the old one. It is not, but Raymond insists. Fell asleep on the couch before the replay finished. Nothing remarkable. Just a day that passed the way days pass when nobody needs anything from you.

The quarterly results came out and Daniel's team got the spotlight again. His consolidation project — my idea, let us be honest — delivered a twelve percent reduction in cross-border processing time. The GM mentioned it in the town hall. Daniel stood up and talked about "collaborative innovation" and "cross-functional alignment." I was sitting in the third row watching him get applause for work that started in my head, over my coffee, in my office.

Nobody cut me off this time. Nobody had to. The story has already been written. He pitched it, he ran it, he gets the credit. And I sat there again, clapping politely, because that is what you do. My wife asked me tonight why I was in such a foul mood. I told her work was tiring. She said I should consider whether this job still gives me what I need. I nearly snapped at her. What does she know about what I need from this job. She runs a catering business out of our kitchen. This is a forty-five person operation across three countries.

But I did not say that. Because somewhere underneath the anger I know she might have a point. This used to be the place where I mattered. Where my decisions moved things. Now I sit in meetings and nod and watch younger people present my ideas and get called innovative. I am not sure what I am holding onto anymore. The title still says Senior Operations Manager. But it is starting to feel like a frame around an empty picture.

Nudge: "What would it look like if you stopped holding onto the title?"

Response: I do not know. That is the honest answer. I have been Kenneth-the-operations-guy for so long I am not sure there is another version underneath. And that thought is worse than the meetings.

The studio smells like turpentine again because I forgot to cap the jar before I left yesterday. Spent six hours on the commission piece for that hotel lobby in Abdoun. Massive canvas, three meters wide, and the client wants "something that feels Middle Eastern but modern." I've heard that phrase so many times it's lost all meaning. I painted geometric patterns, scraped them off, painted loose abstractions over them, scraped those off too. Ended up with something layered that looks like a wall that's been lived on for decades. It might work. I'm too tired to tell.

Drove to the center after to set up for tomorrow's session. The hallway light is still broken so I propped the door open for daylight while I organized supplies. The kids went through all the blue paint last week so I picked up more on the way, the cheap kind because the good acrylics cost too much for how fast they use them. Counted the brushes. We're down to fourteen. I keep forgetting to ask Huda if the center can budget for more or if that's coming out of my pocket again.

The drive home was quiet. Ramadan traffic has died down, the streets were almost empty. I ate leftover mujaddara standing at the kitchen counter and went straight to bed. My shoulders ache from reaching up at the canvas all day. I should stretch more. I never do.

Nudge: "How often do you end up covering those costs yourself?"

Something happened at a dinner at my uncle's house on Friday that I'm still thinking about. His neighbor was there, an older man, very confident in his opinions. He started talking about the Sudanese workers at a construction site near his building. Calling them lazy, saying they were "bringing problems," that the government should stop giving them permits. The usual garbage. My aunt was nodding along. My cousin was on his phone, not paying attention. My uncle said nothing, which for him is the same as agreeing.

I sat there with my fork in my hand and I didn't say a single thing. I knew exactly what I wanted to say. That these men left everything behind and are breaking their bodies on scaffolding in forty-degree heat for wages that wouldn't cover his grocery bill. That my grandmother was a refugee once too. That the least we owe people who have lost their homes is not talking about them like they're pests at a dinner table. I had all of it ready and I just kept eating.

Part of it was exhaustion, I'd been at the studio since seven that morning. Part of it was knowing it wouldn't change his mind. But mostly I think I just didn't want the tension. I didn't want my aunt looking at me that way she does when I "make things difficult." I picked the easy thing. I've been carrying that around for days now and it doesn't feel lighter.

Nudge: "What kept you from saying it?"

Workshop today. One of the boys, Ahmad, who's maybe twelve, has been coming every week since January. He barely spoke for the first month, just painted the same image over and over, a grey building with no windows. Today he painted a garden. Green everywhere, flowers I don't think he's ever seen in real life, a yellow bird. He didn't say anything about it. He just held it up to dry and went to wash his brushes.

I didn't make a fuss. I just told him the yellow was a good choice and helped him find a spot on the drying rack. It was a small thing. But I drove home feeling like something had actually worked, not because of me specifically, just because the space existed for him to get there on his own. That felt right. I'm exhausted but it's the kind of tired I don't mind.

Took the hotel commission money and spent it on new brushes for the studio instead of restocking the center's supplies like I'd planned. The kids need materials more than I need sable brushes. I know that and I did it anyway.

Nudge: "What made you choose the brushes?"

Response: I was standing in the art store and I was tired of always using the cheap ones. Just once I wanted something nice for myself.

My friend Dina made a joke at lunch about how the street cats near Jabal Amman "should all be rounded up." I know she doesn't really mean harm but it landed wrong and I just laughed along.

Nudge: "Why did you laugh?"

Went to the center early to set up before the kids arrived. I'd brought the sable brushes from my studio, the ones I bought with the hotel money. I put them out with the regular supplies and didn't say anything about it. Ahmad used one of them and you could see the difference in how the paint moved. He looked up at me like something had changed and I just shrugged.

Funny how that worked out. I spent four days feeling guilty about buying those brushes and then I brought them to the center anyway. Not because I'm generous or whatever, more because they were sitting in my studio and I kept thinking about fourteen kids sharing broken-handled acrylics. The brushes are just brushes. They belong where they get used. Walked home the long way, past the old city walls. The jasmine is blooming early this year.
Parent-teacher meeting ran late. Most parents didn't show up, same as last month. Spent the rest of the evening marking notebooks and helping Amma sort the kitchen.

Went to the literacy camp in Sultanpur today and I keep thinking about this one girl, maybe seven or eight, who was holding the pencil like she'd never held one before. And honestly she probably hadn't. She was gripping it so tight her knuckles were white and she kept looking at me like she was asking permission to write her own name.

I sat with her for almost an hour. The other volunteers were doing group activities but I just couldn't move on from this kid. Her name is Fatima. She got the first two letters right and then she looked at me and smiled and it was like, okay, this is why I wake up early on Saturdays and take that awful bus that takes ninety minutes one way.

I know this sounds naive or whatever. Didi would say I'm romanticizing things. But I was there today and she wasn't. There's something about being in the room with someone who just learned they can write their own name that makes all the other noise—the salary complaints, Baba asking when I'll "move up," the crumbling school building—feel small. Not irrelevant, just not the point.

Nudge: "What keeps you going back every Saturday?"

Interesting thing happened during morning assembly. One of my Class 3 students, Ravi, came up to me after and said he told his mother about the water pollution lesson I did last week. She apparently asked him to explain it again because their neighborhood has been complaining about the nala running brown. He was so proud of himself, like he'd done something important.

And maybe he did? A nine-year-old going home and talking to his family about why their water is dirty—that feels like something actually working. Not in a big dramatic way, just a small thread pulling in the right direction. I changed nothing about anyone's pipes but now one family is paying attention. That's enough for today.

Nudge: "Do you think those small threads actually add up over time?"

Response: Have to believe they do, otherwise why bother.

Didi called from Bangalore. She's remodelling her flat and asked if I wanted her old furniture shipped to Lucknow. Said no thanks, my room barely fits what I already have. Realized after I hung up that we didn't talk about anything real.

Finished compiling the mid-term attendance data. Our school's retention rate is up from last year, which the principal seemed happy about. Stayed back to help the peon uncle fix a broken desk in my classroom.

Long day. Staff meeting after school ran for two hours and most of it was about budget allocation for the new computer lab. Fine, whatever, that matters too. But then Sharma sir made this comment about how "these children's parents don't even value education, so why are we bending over backwards with extra programs." A few teachers nodded. I just sat there.

I should have said something. These are my students he was talking about. Their parents work twelve-hour shifts at brick kilns and construction sites—of course they can't show up to every PTM. That doesn't mean they don't care. Ravi's mother walked forty minutes to come to the last one. But I kept quiet because Sharma sir is senior and it was the end of a long day and I didn't have the energy to start something. Took the bus home feeling like I'd swallowed something heavy.

Nudge: "What stopped you from pushing back in that moment?"

Response: Honestly just tired. One comment doesn't change anything.

Still thinking about yesterday but also something from today that's bothering me more. Went to the market after school to buy supplies for next week's art project. The stationery shop I usually go to was closed so I went to the bigger one near Hazratganj. More expensive but they had everything I needed.

Here's the thing. There's a local self-help group run by women from the basti near school—they make paper products, notebooks, craft materials. I know about them because one of my students' mothers is involved. They sell at lower prices and the money goes directly back to the community. I've bought from them before. But today I was tired and the Hazratganj shop was right there and I just wanted to get it done fast. So I spent probably twice as much at a chain store when I could have waited one more day and supported the women's group instead.

It's not a huge deal in isolation. Nobody's tracking my purchases. But it's the kind of thing that nags at me because I talk a lot, in my own head at least, about wanting things to be more fair, about wanting the system to work differently. And then when I have an actual tiny concrete chance to put money where my mouth is, I go for the convenient option. Feels hypocritical. The women's group doesn't need my patronage to survive but it's the principle of it. Or maybe I'm overthinking a stationery run, I don't know. I just don't like who I was at that checkout counter today.

Nudge: "Is it really about the stationery or something bigger?"

Response: It's bigger. Whether I actually live this way or just think it.

Week went by fast. Exams are coming so the classroom mood has shifted—less exploration, more revision. I don't love exam season but it's part of the deal. Spent most of the week drilling multiplication tables and grammar rules, which is fine, just not what I find interesting about teaching.

Saturday camp was cancelled because of some local election rally blocking the roads to Sultanpur. So I had the whole day free, which almost never happens. Used it to reorganize my lesson plans for after the exams. Want to do a unit on water and sanitation since the Ravi thing showed me the kids actually carry these conversations home.

Baba asked over dinner if I'd thought about the B.Ed entrance coaching that Didi offered to pay for. He thinks a higher qualification will help me "move to a better school." I told him I like my school. He said he knows, but he worries. I think he genuinely does. It's not like Didi's thing where she's comparing our lives—Baba just wants to know I'll be okay. Told him I'm okay right now and that has to count for something. He nodded and changed the subject to cricket, which is his way of accepting things he doesn't fully understand. I'll take it.

Finished a long translation project today for that small organization documenting water access issues in South Asia. The pay was honestly below market rate but reading through those testimonies, I kept thinking about my grandmother's village where they still walk two kilometers for clean water. Some things matter more than what shows up in my bank account.

My cousin called from Mumbai asking why I don't take more corporate work since my English is good enough. I tried explaining but it felt like talking to a wall. She thinks I'm being foolish, giving away my skills to people who can't pay properly. Maybe she's right about the practical side. But when I close my laptop after a day like today, I feel something she probably wouldn't understand.

The community garden is finally showing real growth. We planted those tomato seedlings with the Syrian family three weeks ago and today Amira brought her daughters to see the little green fruits forming. The oldest one, maybe seven years old, touched the leaves so gently like she was afraid of breaking something precious. I remember that feeling from when I first gardened with my mother on our tiny balcony.

These moments remind me why I spend my Saturdays here instead of picking up extra delivery shifts. Not everything needs a transaction attached to it. Though I'll admit the rent notice sitting on my kitchen counter is making that philosophy harder to hold onto this week. The landlord raised it again and I'm already stretched thin. Still, watching those girls discover where food actually comes from felt like enough today.

Nudge: "You mentioned the rent increase is making your philosophy 'harder to hold onto.' What happens when these two parts of your life feel like they're pulling against each other?"

Response: It terrifies me, honestly. I came here to build a different kind of life than the one that was mapped out for me in Mumbai, where everything is about climbing and earning and comparing. But now I lie awake some nights wondering if I'm just being naive. If my principles are a luxury I can't actually afford. My father used to say that ideals don't fill stomachs, and I hated hearing it, but lately his voice echoes louder. I don't want to become someone who stops caring about the bigger picture just to secure my own small corner. But I also can't keep pretending the practical doesn't matter.

Something small happened during a delivery today that I can't stop thinking about. I was dropping off food at one of those new condo towers downtown, the kind with the marble lobby and the doorman who barely looks at you. The customer came down herself instead of asking me to leave it at the desk. She saw my reusable bag and we ended up talking for five minutes about plastic waste in the ocean. She works for an environmental law firm apparently.

It's easy to feel invisible doing delivery work, just a body moving packages from one place to another. But for those five minutes I was a person with thoughts and concerns that mattered to someone. She gave me her card and said they sometimes need Gujarati translators for community outreach. I probably won't follow up because I'm always hesitant to mix networking with genuine connection. But it reminded me that caring about the world isn't as rare as it sometimes feels. Other people are thinking about these things too.

Nudge: "You said you probably won't follow up because of mixing networking with genuine connection. What feels complicated about that?"

Response: I worry that if I reach out for work, it changes what that conversation was. It becomes transactional instead of just two people recognizing something shared. Maybe that's too precious about it. In Mumbai, everyone networks constantly and there's no shame in it. But I came here partly to escape that feeling of every relationship being measured by what it can do for you. If I contact her for the translation work, am I being practical or am I becoming the thing I was running from?

Read an article this morning about garment factory conditions in Bangladesh that ruined my whole day. I know I can't save everyone. I know my choices, even the careful ones about what I buy and who I work for, are drops in an ocean. But sometimes the weight of knowing what's happening out there feels unbearable. All these people suffering right now while I sit here with my morning chai worrying about my rent.

Called my mother after because I needed to hear her voice. She asked why I sounded sad and I couldn't explain. How do you tell someone that you're grieving for people you've never met, that reading about injustice thousands of kilometers away can sink your whole day? She would tell me to focus on my own life, that I can't carry the world. She means well. But the world is not something outside me that I can choose to put down. We're all connected, whether we see it or not.

Evening now and I'm feeling better. Went back to the garden this afternoon and helped plant cucumber seedlings with Mr. Chen, the retired schoolteacher from the housing complex. He doesn't speak much English but we've developed a language through pointing and smiling and showing each other what to do next. There's something deeply right about working the soil with someone in complete silence.

I think this is what keeps me grounded when the news gets too heavy. Not fixing anything, not solving the big problems, just being present with another person doing something small and generative. The cucumbers will grow whether or not I read about suffering. The children will eat them. And somehow that feels like an answer, even if I can't quite articulate what question it's answering.

Nudge: "You said this feels like an answer to a question you can't articulate. If you tried to put that question into words, what might it sound like?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe I'm just being overly philosophical about putting seeds in dirt. Some things don't need to be analyzed, they just need to be done. My grandmother would say I think too much, that the hands should be busy while the mind rests. Perhaps she was wiser than all my searching.

Third entry today, I know. But I wanted to capture this before I sleep. Amira, the Syrian mother from the garden, sent me a photo through our community group chat. It's her family eating dinner together, and on the table are tomatoes from our garden. Just regular tomatoes, slightly misshapen, nothing special. But the message she wrote said "Thank you for teaching us this. My daughters now understand that food is a gift."

Sometimes I question whether anything I do matters against the scale of problems in the world. The displacement, the inequality, the destruction of the planet. What difference does one small garden make? But then a photo arrives and I remember that we don't measure impact only in statistics. One family eating vegetables they grew themselves. One child learning that nurturing something is possible. These are not solutions to systemic problems, I know that. But maybe they're something else that also matters. I'm grateful tonight. Even with the uncertainty about rent and whether I'm living foolishly. This is the life I chose.

Matthias presented at the seminar today. He used my framing — the institutional capture argument I workshopped in November. Didn't cite me. Didn't even glance my way. Just rolled through the slides like it was all his.

I sat there and said nothing. Prof. Berger was nodding along, which made it worse. She was the one who heard me present that framing first. She knows. Or she doesn't care, which is a different kind of problem.

Afterwards a few people came up to Matthias to congratulate him on "the angle." I stayed in my seat packing up my laptop. Took my time so it wouldn't look like I was leaving in a hurry. I wasn't going to say something petty in front of the group. That's not how it works.

But it's sitting with me now. I keep replaying the moment he said "institutional capture as a downstream effect" and I wanted to interrupt. I just didn't. I don't even know why. Maybe because it would have sounded like sour grapes and I'd come off looking small. So instead I'm here, being small anyway, just quietly.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying something?"

Still thinking about yesterday. I emailed Prof. Berger this morning about the upcoming call for workshop proposals. Partly because I genuinely want to organize one, partly because I needed her to see me being proactive. To remember that I'm the one who builds things in this department.

She responded three hours later with "Let's discuss next week." That's it. No enthusiasm. No "great idea, Lukas." Just a scheduling deflection. Now I have to wait a full week before I even know if she's going to back my proposal or steer it toward someone else.

I know I'm reading too much into an email. People are busy. But when I need something to move forward and the only thing I can do is sit and wait for someone else to say yes — I don't handle it well. I started drafting a second email explaining the scope, then deleted it because I could hear how it would sound. Eager. Pushy. The kind of student who needs reassurance.

So now I'm just stuck. The proposal deadline is March 10th and I can't do anything meaningful until she weighs in. I reorganized my reference library instead. Productive in theory, useless in practice.

Nudge: "Was the workshop the real point of that email?"

Response: Both, honestly. I want to run the workshop. I also want her to think of me first when these things come up. I don't see why those have to be separate motivations.

Meeting with both supervisors today and I'm still fuming a little. We were going over the chapter structure for the dissertation and I had a clear plan — three empirical chapters, the middle one built around the comparative case study I've been developing since October. Prof. Berger agreed with it in our last one-on-one. Today, Dr. Hofer pushed back hard. He wants the comparative chapter folded into an appendix and the middle section replaced with a quantitative replication study.

I argued for maybe five minutes. Laid out why the comparative design was stronger, why it fit the theoretical contribution. He kept saying "the committee will want robustness" and "you'll thank me later." Prof. Berger didn't intervene. She just sat there with her reading glasses on, looking at the outline like it was a menu.

I gave in. Said I'd draft both options and we could revisit. Which is code for: I'll do it his way because fighting him will cost me more than the chapter is worth. He's on two hiring committees I care about.

The worst part is I know the comparative chapter is better work. I know it. And I folded anyway because the politics mattered more than being right. I hate that I made that calculation so fast.

Conference reception last night at the Rathaus. I ended up next to a new postdoc, Clara, who's apparently been hired to work on fiscal governance — which overlaps directly with my dissertation. She introduced herself by explaining what fiscal governance means. Like I was an undergrad at a career fair.

I mentioned my paper and she said "oh interesting, I'll look it up," in the exact tone people use when they won't. Then Prof. Berger came over and introduced me as "one of our PhD students working on something adjacent." Adjacent. I've spent two and a half years on this. Clara nodded politely and moved on to talk to the visiting keynote speaker.

Nudge: "What did 'adjacent' feel like in that moment?"

Response: Like I hadn't built anything yet. Like all the seminars I organized and the papers I've drafted were just... preliminary. Student work. Not the real thing. I know Berger probably didn't mean it that way. But standing next to someone who just got hired into a role I'm working toward, and being introduced as "adjacent" — it felt like a preview of not mattering.

Department funding allocations came out and my travel budget got cut by a third. Meanwhile Matthias got a full conference grant to present in Barcelona. I asked the admin office why and they said "decisions were made at faculty level" which tells me nothing and everything at the same time.

Nudge: "What does 'everything' mean there?"

Response: It means the usual. Resources go to whoever's visible. I need to focus on the proposal deadline anyway — that's a better use of my time than chasing budget decisions I can't influence.

Spent the morning reformatting citations for the replication chapter. Tedious but fine. Had a coffee with Jens from the sociology department who wants to co-organize a reading group next semester — said I could handle logistics and speaker invitations, which I'm already planning out in my head.

Board meeting for the trade association today. One of the newer members, a woman from a Cambodian labour rights group, raised concerns about working conditions at several member factories — specifically about informal workers who sort e-waste without proper protective gear. She had photos. She had data from a university study. She asked the board to issue a formal statement and require audits before renewing factory memberships.

I didn't say anything. I just sat there. A few other board members started pushing back immediately — talking about how the data was old, how conditions had improved, how public statements would hurt investment confidence in the sector. The usual deflections. And I know they're partly right, some of those factories have made genuine improvements. But the photos were recent. The data wasn't old. I could see the timestamps.

The thing is, two of those factories are long-standing clients of mine. We process their scrap. If I backed the statement, it would put those relationships at risk. Not just financially — personally. I've known these owners for over a decade. We've eaten at each other's homes. It's not as simple as just raising your hand and saying yes, we should hold them accountable. There are layers to this. But I keep replaying her face when nobody supported her motion. She looked like she expected it. That's worse somehow — that she wasn't even surprised.

Nudge: "What stopped you from backing her?"

Response: The relationships. Twenty years of built trust. You don't blow that up in a board meeting.

My son called from Jakarta. His NGO just secured funding to expand their mangrove restoration project into two new provinces. I could hear it in his voice — the energy, the belief that what he's doing actually matters. I told him I was proud. I meant it completely.

After we hung up I transferred some money to the foundation account. Not a huge amount, but enough to cover the clean-water pump installation we've been delaying in Siem Reap because the supplier raised prices. My wife asked why I didn't just wait until next quarter when cash flow is better. I told her some things shouldn't wait. She gave me that look she gives when she thinks I'm being impulsive, but she processed the transfer anyway.

Nudge: "What was it about that call that made you act right away?"

Response: Hearing him talk about the mangroves reminded me why I started the foundation. Sometimes you forget.

Flew back from a factory visit in Penang. The new processing line looks good, efficiency is way up, costs are down. Everyone was pleased. On the drive to the airport the plant manager mentioned casually that they'd switched to a cheaper chemical solvent for circuit board stripping. Cheaper and, I suspect, not properly rated for ventilation in that building. I could smell it from the parking lot.

I didn't ask about it. I could have. I had forty minutes in the car and I know enough about chemical handling to ask the right questions. But I was tired and the deal was done and bringing it up would have meant delaying the whole partnership review. I chose convenience. The flight home felt longer than usual.

People keep asking why I don't just retire and run the foundation full-time. As if it's that simple. The business funds everything — the foundation, my children's projects, the scholarships. Without it there's nothing to give.

Nudge: "What would you lose if you let it go?"

Response: Not just income. The ability to actually do something about the problems I see. Money is leverage.

Saturday morning, went to the hawker centre near our old Toa Payoh flat. The uncle who runs the wonton noodle stall has been there since my kids were small, must be almost thirty years now. We talked for a while. He's worried about his lease renewal, says the rents keep going up and the younger hawkers can't make it work anymore. Whole stretch of stalls might close.

I started thinking about whether the trade association could do something — not directly but maybe model something. We talk a lot about industry sustainability but it's always about supply chains and compliance frameworks. Nobody talks about what happens to the communities around these supply chains. The hawker uncle isn't e-waste, obviously, but it's the same pattern. Small people squeezed by systems that don't see them. I should bring this up at the next meeting. Or maybe I'll just sit there again. Don't know.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to actually bring it up?"

Response: Probably nothing. The agenda is always packed and nobody wants to hear philosophy from the recycling guy.

Dinner with old university friends tonight. David made a comment about how the migrant worker housing situation in Singapore has been "overblown by the media." Two others at the table agreed. I remember David saying something similar during the dormitory outbreaks a few years ago.

I didn't push back. I changed the subject to the new hawker centre opening in Bukit Merah. The conversation moved on. My wife squeezed my hand under the table — she knows what I think about the worker housing issue. We've donated to a couple of migrant worker charities quietly. But sitting there listening to David dismiss it, and just letting it pass — that sat with me on the drive home.

Chose a cheaper shipping route for the Cambodia pump equipment today. Faster and half the cost, but it goes through a freight company I know cuts corners on packaging for rural deliveries. Easier for me. Maybe not for whoever unpacks a damaged pump at the other end.

Nudge: "Worth the savings?"

Took the morning to deal with the new shift scheduling. HR wants us to finalize the December roster by Thursday which means I had to pull two supervisors into a room and sort through everyone's leave requests. Nothing dramatic, just the usual back and forth about who covers what. One of the guys asked for the entire last week of December off and I approved it because he hasn't taken time in months. Didn't think much of it at the time.

Lunch was at my desk again. Brought lentil soup from home but it had gone lukewarm by the time I got to it. I keep telling myself I'll start eating in the break room like a normal person but there's always one more email. After lunch I had the monthly safety review call with the Zarqa site, which went fine, nothing flagged. Then spent the rest of the afternoon updating inventory projections.

Drove home the long way because traffic on the highway was backed up near the Seventh Circle. Put on some Fairuz which was nice. Got home, heated up what was left of last night's mansaf, and sat on the balcony for maybe ten minutes before I started going through tomorrow's agenda on my phone.

Nudge: "That ten minutes on the balcony — what pulled you back inside?"

Response: Nothing dramatic. I just remembered I hadn't confirmed the forklift maintenance slot for Thursday. Once I picked up the phone it was over.

Still up, can't really settle. After that last entry I went and made tea and sat back on the balcony, properly this time, no phone. And it was good for a few minutes, the air was cool and I could hear the mosque down the street and I was just sitting there with my tea. But then this thing started where I'm thinking about how I approved Fadi's leave for December which means I'll need to cover his shifts myself or find someone, and suddenly the tea doesn't taste like anything anymore.

It's stupid. I literally just wanted to sit and drink tea. That's all I was doing. And I couldn't let myself have it for more than five minutes before my head started making it into a problem that I wasn't being productive. I don't even know who I'm performing for at eleven at night on my own balcony.

Nudge: "Who taught you that sitting still needs a reason?"

Response: Everybody gets like this. It's not that deep.
Third time opening this today. Just realized I never actually finished the tea. Found it cold on the railing. Going to bed.

Bought knafeh on the way home from the Zarqa site. Ate it in the car in the parking lot before going upstairs, still warm. Didn't check my phone once.

Nudge: "What made you stay in the car for that?"

Everyone at work acts like I don't know how to take a break. My sister called on Friday and said Mama told her I looked tired at dinner, and then today Rami, one of my supervisors, made some comment about how I should use my own leave balance before telling everyone else to use theirs. And I get it, fine, they have a point on paper. But it's not like I'm some robot who doesn't want to rest. I do. I just can't flip a switch and turn off the part of my brain that keeps running through what needs to get done.

I was genuinely tired today, like the kind of tired where you sit down and your whole body feels heavy. And I thought about going home early, it was a quiet afternoon, nothing urgent. But then I started thinking about how that would look, leaving at three when my team is there until five. Even though nobody would care. Even though I tell my own people to go home early when it's slow. I couldn't give myself the same thing I'd give anyone else without it feeling like I was getting away with something.

So I stayed. Answered emails that could have waited. Drove home in the dark. Made rice and ate it standing in the kitchen. I know this is a pattern. I just don't know what I'm supposed to do differently when the guilt is louder than the tiredness.

Nudge: "You'd send Rami home in a heartbeat. What makes your rest different?"

Response: Because if I rest and something goes wrong, it's my fault. If they rest, I'm the one who covered. That's the deal I made with myself somewhere.

My cousin had a gathering and there was good food and music and I was looking forward to it all week. Sat there the whole time thinking about a procurement deadline on Monday. Left early and felt worse than if I hadn't gone at all.

Something about today left me flat and I can't quite shake it. We had a cross-functional sync, the kind where everyone brings their updates and pretends to care about everyone else's. I'd prepared a slide on our Q4 handoff plan — tight, clean, the numbers right there. Got two sentences in and Marianne from commercial just started talking over me. Not even a segue, just launched into her own thing like I hadn't been mid-sentence.

The worst part is nobody said anything. Not Pieter, not Jan-Willem, nobody. The meeting chair just let it flow and asked her a follow-up question. My slide was still up on the screen. I sat there staring at it. I could have interrupted her back, could have said "I wasn't finished," but I was so caught off guard I just waited. And then the moment was gone. We moved to the next agenda item.

It's the kind of thing that shouldn't bother me this much but I've been replaying it all evening. I keep thinking about what I should have done. Maybe it's because I'm tired. Maybe I'm reading into it. But if it were someone else — if it were Thomas or Sven — nobody would have talked over them. I know that for a fact.

Nudge: "What do you think made it so hard to push back?"

Response: It wasn't about pushing back, really. I just think meetings like that should have a basic level of order. If I'm presenting, let me present. It's not complicated. The chair should have handled it.

Standard Friday. Had the weekly with my directs, went through capacity, flagged the resource gap on the warehouse integration project. Nothing dramatic. Sent a summary to Pieter afterwards like I always do. He replied with a thumbs-up emoji, which tells me absolutely nothing about whether he actually read it.

Lunch was fine. Grabbed a broodje with Stefan from finance. He mentioned they're hiring a new FP&A lead and wanted to know if I had any opinions on the JD. I gave him a few pointers — told him to make the reporting line clearer because last time the role was too ambiguous and nobody respected whoever sat in it. He seemed to take it on board. Small thing, but at least someone asks.

Nudge: "Does it land differently when people actually ask?"

Response: Of course it does. If someone values your input enough to come find you at lunch, that's real. A thumbs-up on Teams is just someone clearing their inbox. But it's not like I'm keeping score. Stefan and I go back a while, that's all.

Spent half of Thursday chasing a sign-off from legal on the new vendor contract. I'd sent the redlines Monday, followed up Wednesday, and then Thursday morning I walked over to Anneke's desk and she hadn't even opened the attachment. Just said she'd been "swamped" and would get to it "when she could." I asked if there was a way to escalate and she looked at me like I'd insulted her grandmother.

So now I'm sitting on a contract I can't execute, the vendor's account manager has emailed me twice asking for a timeline, and I have to go back to my team and tell them we're delayed because someone in another department can't open a PDF. The whole thing makes me feel like I'm running a team with no real authority over the things that actually affect our deadlines. I can plan all I want — doesn't matter if someone else holds the pen.

Really frustrated tonight. Had a project review with the steering committee — the one Pieter chairs, with Marianne, Thomas, and two of the directors. I'd built out the entire rollback scenario for the platform migration, spent the weekend on the risk matrix, mapped out every dependency. Came in ready.

Thomas disagreed with the timeline. Said we should push back the cutover by two sprints. I explained why that would blow up the downstream handoffs and showed the cascade impact. He just shrugged and said "better safe." The directors nodded along with him, didn't ask me a single follow-up. Pieter said "let's go with Thomas's view for now and revisit in January."

For now. Like my analysis was a suggestion. I didn't fight it because what's the point — they'd already decided. Thomas has been here longer, has a louder voice in those rooms, and people just default to his read. I sat through the rest of the meeting barely listening. Kept thinking: I did the work, I had the data, and none of it mattered because the room had already picked a side before I even opened my mouth.

Went home and just sat on the couch for an hour. Didn't even eat properly. It's not about the timeline itself — I can live with the delay. It's that feeling of preparing something thorough and watching it get waved away. Like the effort was irrelevant.

Nudge: "Is it the outcome or being overruled that stings more?"

Response: Look, I said it myself — the delay is fine. It's the process. If you're going to ask me to do the analysis, at least engage with it. Don't just nod at the person who says "better safe" and call it a day. That's not how you run a steering committee. But I suppose that's how things work around here.

Had a strange moment today. HR sent around the invitations for the annual leadership development programme — the one that feeds into the senior management pipeline. Last year I wasn't eligible because of tenure. This year I definitely am. But when the list of nominees came out, my name wasn't on it. Thomas was on it. Marianne was on it. Even Daan, who joined six months after me and runs a smaller team.

I asked my line manager about it and he said nominations go through "calibration" with the directors and he'd "look into it." Which is code for: I don't know and I won't push it. He said it as if it were a minor admin glitch, like a wrong room booking. I stood there nodding, said thanks, walked back to my desk.

I keep going back and forth. Part of me thinks it's a genuine oversight — maybe my name slipped through, maybe someone forgot to tick a box. But then I think about the steering committee last week, about Marianne talking over me, about how Pieter barely registers my updates, and it starts to feel like a pattern. Like somewhere along the line, the people who decide these things stopped seeing me as someone on the upward track.

I don't want to be the person who reads slights into everything. But I also don't want to be the person who just lets things pass and wakes up three years from now still in the same chair, wondering what happened.

Nudge: "What would pushing on this actually look like?"

Response: That's the thing I keep turning over. I could email the HR business partner directly, ask for the criteria, make a case. I've done that kind of thing before — when I wanted the title change, I didn't wait for anyone to offer it. I put together a one-pager and walked into my manager's office. But this feels different.
