The title was something I could argue on merit. This feels more like... I need people to already think of me a certain way, and if they don't, no one-pager is going to change that. It's the kind of thing you can't just demand. And that's what makes it worse, honestly.

Keep circling back to the meeting from Monday. Different meeting this time, ops standup. Clara was presenting the warehouse KPI dashboard she'd been working on — my idea originally, I flagged it in September and asked her to build it out. She did a good job, I'll say that. Clean design, right metrics. But when she presented it to the wider ops group, she introduced it as her initiative. "I noticed we had a gap in visibility so I put this together."

I didn't correct her. Maybe I should have. But it would have looked petty, right? Standing up in a standup and saying "actually, I told you to do that." Nobody wants to be that manager. So I just sat there, and people praised her for the initiative, and Pieter said something like "great ownership, Clara," and I thought: that was mine.

It's not about credit exactly, or maybe it is, I don't know. It's more like — I set the direction, I identified the problem, I allocated the resource, and the visible output gets attributed to someone else. And the thing is, Clara probably doesn't even realize she did it. She's not scheming. She just stood up and told the story from her perspective. But her perspective conveniently started after I handed her the brief.

Small thing. Shouldn't matter. But it does. Especially this week, after the nomination list and the steering committee and all of it. Feels like I'm becoming invisible in my own team.

Monday morning. Had a one-on-one with Daan — he's been struggling with a resource conflict between two of his workstreams and wanted to talk it through. I helped him map out the trade-offs, walked him through the prioritisation framework I use, and by the end of it he had a clear plan. He thanked me and said he wished more people explained things the way I do. That landed well.

Afterwards I realised that's the version of my job I actually like. Not the steering committees, not the nomination lists, not the politics of who gets heard in which room. Just someone coming to me with a problem, me helping them think it through, and both of us walking away with something useful. I know the other stuff matters — it does — but today reminded me there's a part of this I'm actually good at that has nothing to do with who's watching.

Nudge: "Has it always been like that — the two sides?"

Response: More or less, yes. The helping part has always come naturally. The other part — the positioning, making sure the right people notice — that's something I taught myself. I used to think I needed both to move forward. Maybe I still do. But today was a reminder that one of them actually fills me up and the other just drains me. Doesn't change what I need to do, but it's good to notice, I think.

Spent the whole day on vendor calls. Ate leftover dal at my desk and didn't even heat it up. Just want to lie down.

Nudge: "What kind of tired is this — body or head?"

Response: Both, honestly. The kind where even picking a show to watch feels like work.

Took twenty minutes after lunch to sit on the balcony with a cup of chai. Not even doing anything — just sitting, watching the street below, feeling the breeze. It was genuinely nice for about ten minutes before I started doing math in my head about the delivery timelines I should be checking.

I don't know why I can't just have the chai. It was twenty minutes. My co-founder takes hour-long gym breaks and nobody blinks. But when I sit still, my own brain starts pulling up spreadsheets. Like I haven't done enough today to deserve twenty minutes of doing nothing.

Nudge: "Your co-founder gets an hour but you can't take twenty?"

Couldn't sleep last night because I was so wound up from a client call that went badly. By 2 AM I was just lying there staring at the ceiling fan, and the obvious thing would have been to get up, make something warm, watch something stupid on my phone, let myself come down from it. But instead I just lay there telling myself I needed to sleep because tomorrow was going to be another long day and I couldn't afford to be foggy.

And the thing is, I know forcing sleep doesn't work. I know I would have been better off getting up and doing something comforting for twenty minutes. But some part of me kept translating "get up and do something nice for yourself" into "you're being weak, you can't even handle a bad call without needing to baby yourself."

So I just lay there for another hour and a half, exhausted but not sleeping, not resting, not doing anything useful either. The worst of all options. Woke up foggy anyway. Amma would have told me to drink warm milk and stop overthinking, and she would have been right.

Ravi invited me to this rooftop thing his friends were doing — live music, good food, casual. I actually wanted to go. But I had a pitch deck that needed reworking by Monday and I told myself it made more sense to stay in and finish it. Which it did, technically. The pitch is important. I'm not going to pretend otherwise.

But I sat at my desk all evening and the deck took twice as long as it should have because I kept checking Ravi's stories and seeing everyone having a good time. The tandoori looked incredible. Someone was playing guitar and people were just sitting around talking. Nothing fancy. The exact kind of night I would have loved.

I finished the deck around midnight and it's fine. It's solid. But the flat was so quiet and I just felt flat myself. Like I'd done the responsible thing and the reward for being responsible was sitting alone in a silent apartment eating cold chapati at midnight wondering what the point of all this work is if I never actually enjoy anything.

Nudge: "Was the deck actually urgent or did it just feel that way?"

Normal morning. Replied to emails, had a standup call that went longer than necessary because Priya wanted to talk through the onboarding flow again. Grabbed idli from the canteen downstairs. The sambar was better than usual today.

Afternoon was mostly spreadsheet work. Nothing dramatic. Stepped out for a coffee around four and ran into the guy who runs the print shop next door. We talked about cricket for ten minutes. Came back and finished up.

Writing again because I left something out earlier. After the coffee and the cricket chat I actually felt good — like, weirdly good for a Tuesday. And I sat back down at my desk and instead of opening the spreadsheet I just sat there for a few minutes not doing anything. Not on my phone, not thinking about work. Just sort of enjoying the fact that the chai was good and the conversation was easy and the afternoon light through the window was warm.

Then I caught myself and opened the spreadsheet immediately, like I'd been caught doing something wrong. Nobody was watching. Nobody cares. But the instinct was there — that I was wasting time, that good feelings are a distraction, that if I'm not producing something then I'm falling behind. It's a stupid reflex and I know it's stupid but knowing doesn't seem to help.

Nudge: "What were you falling behind on, exactly?"

Response: Nothing specific. That's the honest answer. It's not about an actual deadline — it's just this background hum that says I should always be doing more.

Third time writing today, which is unusual for me. I think the afternoon thing is still sitting with me. Had dinner — made egg curry, nothing special but the kitchen smelled good and I ate slowly for once instead of standing at the counter scrolling through Slack.

It occurs to me that the small things I actually enjoy — cooking, eating properly, having a pointless conversation about cricket — none of them take very long. It's not like I'm asking for a vacation. I just want to eat my food without feeling like I'm stealing time from something more important. That shouldn't be complicated.

Long day. The investor meeting got pushed to Thursday which should feel like breathing room but mostly just means I'll be anxious for two extra days. Had a working lunch with Priya going over the numbers. She brought biryani from that place near her flat which was good — proper Hyderabadi style with the rice done right. We ate while reviewing the P&L which felt wrong somehow, like the biryani deserved better attention than that.

After work I was too tired to cook so I ordered in and ate on the couch watching something forgettable. The flat was quiet. Not in a bad way, just quiet. I kept thinking about how my father used to come home from the office and sit in his chair for exactly fifteen minutes before dinner, not talking, not reading, just sitting. My mother never interrupted those fifteen minutes. I used to think it was strange but now I think I understand it — he needed a gap between the work version of himself and the home version.

I don't really have that gap. The work version and the home version blur into each other because I work from the same room I sleep in. Maybe that's part of it. Maybe if there were clearer edges I wouldn't feel so guilty about the in-between moments.

Spent all morning fixing the matching algorithm instead of calling back the three tutors who said they might quit. Keep choosing the code over the people. Mom called twice, didn't pick up. The platform won't matter if nobody's left to teach on it.

Thinking about yesterday. I tell myself I built this thing to help kids but half the time I'm just hunched over my laptop ignoring everyone. Soyeon asked if I was okay and I said yeah, fine. I wasn't fine. I was annoyed she interrupted me.

Nudge: "Who are you building this for right now?"

One of the students, Minjun, sent me a message through the app. Just a short thing, maybe three lines. Said his math grade went from a D to a B minus and that his tutor Hyunwoo explains things in a way that doesn't make him feel stupid. I read it on the subway and my eyes got hot. I looked up at the ceiling so nobody would see.

This is why I do it. Not the pitch decks, not the investor meetings I keep fumbling. This kid in Incheon who doesn't feel stupid anymore. I saved the screenshot. I'm going to look at it on the bad days.

Nudge: "What do the bad days look like?"

Response: Days where nobody replies to my messages. Where I eat convenience store kimbap for dinner again and wonder if I should've just stayed in school like everyone else. Where I check our user numbers and they haven't moved.

Same day, later. Had dinner with Dad. He drove all the way from Incheon, which means he had something to say. We went to the jjigae place near Hongdae station, the one we used to go to when I was still enrolled and he'd visit on weekends.

He didn't bring up school right away. Asked about the platform, how many students we have now. I told him forty-seven active pairs. He nodded, did the math in his head the way he always does. "That's forty-seven kids whose parents can't afford hagwon," he said. Then he got quiet for a while.

Eventually he said your mother worries. I said I know. He said she doesn't understand why you couldn't do both, school and this. I told him it's not that kind of thing, you can't half-build something like this. He looked at his soup and said I know, I'm just telling you what she says.

We walked to the subway together after. He didn't bring up re-enrolling. That's new. At the turnstile he put his hand on my shoulder for a second, didn't say anything, and went through. I stood there like an idiot watching him go down the escalator. I think that was him saying he gets it. Or at least that he's trying to.

My old classmate Eunji posted about getting into Samsung's graduate program. Good for her. Everyone in the group chat congratulating her. I didn't say anything. I'm not jealous, I just don't have anything to say about it. My path looks nothing like that and I'm fine with it.

Hyunwoo, the tutor Minjun likes so much, told me he's graduating next month and can't keep volunteering. He was apologetic about it, kept saying he'd try to find a replacement. I told him it's fine, don't worry about it, we'll figure it out. We'll figure it out is what I say when I have no idea what to do.

I spent the evening going through the waitlist of potential tutors. There are eleven people who signed up in the last month. I messaged all of them. Three bounced, two said they're too busy now, one asked if we pay. Five didn't reply. So that's great.

The thing is I could pay tutors. I have enough from the freelance work to cover maybe two or three stipends. But then I'd have to charge the families or find a sponsor and both of those options make me feel like I'm turning this into something it's not supposed to be. I started this because the whole point was that it's free. That nobody has to qualify or prove they deserve help. But I can't keep bleeding money either. Soyeon says I'm being stubborn about it. She might be right but I don't want to hear it from her right now.

I keep thinking about Minjun. Who's going to explain things to him in a way that doesn't make him feel stupid.

Nudge: "What would you tell Minjun if he asked why his tutor left?"

Response: That people move on and it's not his fault. That I'd find someone just as good. I'd be lying about the second part but he doesn't need to carry that. He's fourteen.

Haven't slept properly in three days. Freelance deadline tomorrow, two tutor spots still empty, Mom sent a long KakaoTalk message about my cousin's wedding that I know is really about me being twenty-two and having nothing stable. I want to reply to her but I don't know how to explain any of this without starting a fight.

Nudge: "What part of it would start the fight?"

Response: The part where I say I don't regret leaving school. She hears that as me saying her life as a teacher wasn't enough. That's not what I mean but it always lands that way.

Found a replacement tutor for Minjun. Engineering student at Yonsei, seems solid. Stayed up until 2am onboarding her. Soyeon brought me coffee this morning without me asking. I almost cried over a cup of coffee. I think I need to be better at letting people help me.
Long day. Back-to-back calls with the ops team, then a vendor demo that went nowhere. Ordered biryani from that place near Koramangala and ate it standing at my desk between Slack messages.

Good morning for once. Walked into the all-hands and the team actually went quiet when I started talking. Not in a forced way — more like they were waiting for what I'd say because it mattered. I laid out the Q3 roadmap, handled the questions without anyone second-guessing the direction, and wrapped it in thirty minutes flat. Even Priya, who always has a follow-up objection, just nodded.

After the meeting, Arjun from engineering came up and said he liked the framing on the enterprise pivot. Didn't ask for it, didn't fish for it. He just said it. Small thing, but I've been pushing this pivot for two months against a lot of internal skepticism, and hearing someone voluntarily say it made sense — that hit differently.

I know some people think I care too much about being the one steering the ship, and maybe I do. But days like today remind me why I left a comfortable desk to do this. I didn't want to execute someone else's vision forever. I wanted the room to look at me and know that I'm the one making the call. Today they did, and it felt right.

Spoke too soon this morning. Investor call this evening, and Vikram — my own co-founder — cut me off mid-pitch to "clarify" my revenue projections. The investor looked at him, not me, for the rest of the call. I didn't say anything.

Nudge: "You let that slide — what held you back?"

Spent half the week waiting on this one decision. We need approval from the enterprise client's procurement team to move forward with the pilot, and they keep saying "by end of week" and then nothing. I've sent three follow-up emails, each one a little more carefully worded than the last because you can't sound desperate, you can't sound pushy, you just have to sound like you're casually checking in even though the entire Q3 plan hinges on this one green light from someone I've never met.

My Amma called yesterday and asked how business is going. I told her great, growing fast. Didn't mention that I'm sitting here refreshing my inbox like an intern waiting for a response from a mid-level procurement officer named Suresh who probably doesn't even know what our product does. I'm the CEO of this company and I can't move my own roadmap forward because some guy in a cubicle in Pune hasn't gotten around to clicking approve.

The worst part is Vikram doesn't seem bothered. He said "these things take time" like that's an acceptable answer. Maybe for him it is. He's the CTO, he can go write code while we wait. I'm the one who has to explain to the board why the pilot hasn't started yet. I'm the one who looks like he can't close.

Nudge: "What's worse — the waiting or who you become while you wait?"

Had a strategy disagreement with Vikram again, this time about pricing. I wanted to go aggressive — undercut the market, lock in the enterprise accounts, worry about margins later once we have the install base. He wanted a premium positioning play, said undercutting makes us look desperate. We went back and forth for an hour in the conference room and I could tell the team outside could hear us through the glass.

The thing is, I still think I'm right. I've done the competitor analysis, I know what Freshworks did in their early days, I know how this market works. But Vikram had the numbers laid out differently and the team started leaning his way. Nisha from sales said "I think the premium angle is easier to pitch," and once she said that, it was over. I agreed to his approach because fighting it further would have made me look like I was digging in out of ego rather than strategy.

But sitting here now, what sticks with me isn't even the pricing decision. It's the moment I saw the room shift. It's the feeling of having built this company, having recruited every person in that room, and watching them choose someone else's direction over mine. My father ran his garment business for twenty-five years and nobody in his shop would have contradicted him publicly like that. Different era, I know. But I keep thinking about it.

Went to a founder networking event in Indiranagar last night. This guy from some Web3 startup — couldn't have been older than twenty-four — asked me "so what stage are you at?" and when I said Series A, he actually said "oh, still early then" and turned to talk to someone else. Like I was a booth he'd already walked past. I've been running this company for three years, managed a team twice his size before he probably finished college, and I'm "still early."

I know it shouldn't bother me. I know the kid was probably just performing for the room. But I drove home and sat in my car for a while before going up to the flat. Meera asked why I was late and I said traffic. I didn't want to explain that a stranger made me feel like I haven't done enough. Because I have. I just can't figure out why it still gets to me.

Nudge: "What would 'enough' actually look like for you?"
